docs.text: One's strength is ultimately limited. This book was written in my spare time after staying up late amidst heavy research, so imperfections are inevitable. Additionally, since I specialize in computer applications, many courses emphasize practical applications with less focus on mathematics, theoretical CS, and advanced algorithms. If you have rich self-study experience in other fields, feel free to submit a Pull Request or contact me via email (su2489544776@outlook.com).

docs.title: About study groups
docs.text: This book supports page commenting. If you want to self-study a particular course, create a group chat (QQ or WeChat) and post a comment below the corresponding course page, specifying your learning goals and how to join. Additionally, many friends have established study groups in Issues — feel free to join them directly.

docs.title: Buy the author a coffee
docs.text: This book is completely open-source and free. If you find this project helpful, consider giving the repository a star or buying the author a coffee.

docs.title: A Reference Learning Plan
docs.text: This is not a rigid curriculum to follow strictly, but a learning roadmap tailored to the needs of CS students in the AI era. Everyone's background, goals, and schedule differ — adapt it to your own situation.

docs.title: Design Principles
docs.text:
Foundation first, AI acceleration second. Traditional CS fundamentals (data structures, algorithms, operating systems, computer networks) remain hard currency in the AI era. AI tools can accelerate your learning but cannot replace your understanding of the basics.

Balance theory and practice. Every course recommends video lectures (theory) + labs/projects (practice). Watching videos without doing projects means you'll forget everything.

AI tools throughout. From freshman year, integrate AI-assisted programming, prompt engineering, and similar tools into your daily learning — don't treat them as isolated courses.

docs.title: Phase 1: Freshman Year — Building Foundations
docs.text: Goal: Build programming and math foundations, establish good study habits.

docs.title: Fall Semester
docs.text:
Topic Recommended Resources Python basics Python Official Tutorial C programming Harvard CS50 Calculus I MIT 18.01 Linear algebra 3Blue1Brown Essence of Linear Algebra + MIT 18.06

Practice: Solve 1-2 easy LeetCode problems daily. Use AI as an assistant, not as an answer machine.

docs.title: Spring Semester
docs.text:
Topic Recommended Resources Data structures & algorithms UC Berkeley CS61B Calculus II MIT 18.02 Probability & statistics MIT 6.041 Git & GitHub Git section + GitHub Tutorial

Practice: Complete all CS61B labs and projects. Start using Git to manage your code repositories.

Summer recommendations: - Study Harvard CS50 for consolidation - Build small Python projects (web scraper, data analysis, simple game) - Get initial exposure to AI programming tools (Copilot, ChatGPT)

docs.title: Phase 2: Sophomore Year — Core CS + AI Introduction
docs.text: Goal: Master core CS courses and start exploring AI-related topics.

docs.title: Fall Semester
docs.text:
Topic Recommended Resources Computer architecture UC Berkeley CS61C Operating systems MIT 6.S081 Introduction to ML Andrew Ng Machine Learning AI-assisted programming AI Coding section

Practice: Complete CS61C RISC-V labs and MIT 6.S081 xv6 labs. Start using Claude Code or Cursor in real projects.

docs.title: Spring Semester
docs.text:
Topic Recommended Resources Computer networks Stanford CS144 Database systems CMU 15-445 Deep learning Andrew Ng Deep Learning Specialization Prompt engineering Prompt Engineering section

Practice: Implement a B+ tree index for a simple database; complete an image classification project with PyTorch.

Summer recommendations: - Find a summer internship or contribute to open source - Go deep on an AI application area (NLP/CV/Agents) - Learn Docker and basic DevOps skills

docs.title: Phase 3: Junior Year — AI Specialization + Career Prep
docs.text: Goal: Specialize in your chosen direction while preparing for graduate school or job market.

docs.title: Fall Semester
docs.text:
Topic Recommended Resources Distributed systems MIT 6.824 Reinforcement learning David Silver RL Course RAG systems RAG section Agent development Agent section

Practice: Complete a full AI project (RAG knowledge base, multi-agent system). Start preparing for entrance exams if applicable.

docs.title: Spring Semester
docs.text:
Topic Recommended Resources LLM application engineering AI Engineering section AI safety AI Safety section Compilers (elective) Stanford CS143 or Crafting Interpreters

Practice: Build a portfolio (GitHub repos + project demos). Entrance exam prep at full intensity. Job seekers start Leetcode grinding and interview prep.

docs.title: Phase 4: Senior Year — Capstone and Career
docs.text:
Goal: Complete capstone project, determine career direction.

  • Graduate school track: Prepare for interviews, contact advisors early, read papers in your target area
  • Job track: System design interview prep, continue deepening your tech stack
  • Study abroad track: Prepare application materials, accumulate research experience

docs.title: About the CS Entrance Exam (408)
docs.text:
If you plan to take the Chinese CS graduate entrance exam, these four subjects each account for roughly 25 points:

Subject Points Recommended Course Data Structures ~25 UC Berkeley CS61B Computer Architecture ~25 UC Berkeley CS61C Operating Systems ~25 MIT 6.S081 Computer Networks ~25 Stanford CS144

After completing the above courses, use domestic exam prep materials (e.g., Wangdao series) for targeted review. International courses help you understand principles; exam prep materials help you pass the exam — the combination works best.

docs.title: Principles for Using AI Tools
docs.text:
1. AI is an amplifier, not a replacement. AI helps you write code faster, but responsibility for understanding the code remains yours.
  2. Understand first, then use. Don't use AI to generate code you don't understand. When you encounter something you don't grasp, ask AI to explain the principle.
  3. Use AI to learn, not to do. Have AI explain concepts, translate documents, and summarize papers — but thinking and decision-making must be your own.
  4. Keep your coding hands sharp. Even with AI tools, regularly write code by hand to maintain sensitivity to syntax and algorithms.

This plan is based on the author's personal learning experience and discussions with multiple professors and senior students. Since the author's focus is on applied practice, coverage of theoretical CS and mathematics is more limited. If you have suggestions, please reach out via email.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: Andrew Ng Agentic AI Series、LangGraph Official Documentation、Anthropic Building Effective Agents
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, understanding of LLM API calls, basic understanding of RAG concepts
  • Programming language: Python
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 30+

AI Agents are the most central direction in AI application development today. If RAG solves "what the model knows," Agents solve "what the model does" — they can autonomously plan, use tools, and execute multi-step tasks, not just answer questions.

Core components of an Agent system: - LLM (brain): Responsible for reasoning and decision-making - Tools (hands): Search, code execution, API calls, file operations, etc. - Memory (notebook): Short-term (conversation history) + Long-term (persistent knowledge) - Planning (chain of thought): Decomposing complex tasks into executable steps

Andrew Ng repeatedly emphasized in his 2024-2025 course series: Agentic workflows are the most important paradigm shift in AI application development. Moving from simple single API calls to Agents that can autonomously complete multi-step tasks is a capability every AI engineer must master.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Course: Andrew Ng - AI Agents in LangGraph — Free short course, hands-on with the LangGraph framework
  • Documentation: LangGraph Documentation — The most mainstream multi-agent orchestration framework
  • Article: Anthropic - Building Effective Agents — Anthropic's official Agent design guide, required reading

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Beginner (understanding core Agent concepts):

  • Andrew Ng - Agentic Design Patterns (4 courses) — Covers Reflection, Tool Use, Planning, and Multi-Agent patterns, highly recommended
  • Lilian Weng - LLM Powered Autonomous Agents — One of the most comprehensive Agent technical surveys
  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in LLMs — The original ReAct paper, understand the reasoning+action loop paradigm

Framework practice (pick one to go deep):

  • LangGraph — State-graph-based Agent orchestration framework, supports loops, conditional branches, human-in-the-loop, the most flexible framework available
  • CrewAI — Multi-agent collaboration framework with clear role division, quick to get started
  • AutoGen (Microsoft) — Microsoft's open-source multi-agent conversation framework
  • OpenAI Agents SDK — OpenAI's official Agent SDK, lightweight
  • smolagents (HuggingFace) — Minimalist Agent framework, small codebase, great for learning principles

Tool Use (Tool Use / Function Calling):

  • OpenAI Function Calling Guide — Understand how LLMs call external tools
  • LangChain Tools & Toolkits — Rich collection of pre-built tools
  • MCP (Model Context Protocol) — Anthropic's standardized tool protocol, the most important Agent infrastructure of 2025

Multi-Agent Systems:

  • Andrew Ng - Multi-Agent Collaboration — CrewAI framework + multi-agent collaboration
  • AutoGen Agent Chat — Multi-agent conversation and collaboration
  • MetaGPT — Agent system simulating multi-role software company collaboration

Memory Systems:

  • LangMem (LangChain Memory) — LangChain's official long-term memory solution
  • Mem0 — Intelligent memory layer designed specifically for AI Agents
  • Letta (MemGPT) — Brings OS memory management concepts into Agent memory

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Agent development has a steep learning curve. Recommended path: first understand the ReAct paradigm (reasoning+action loop) → build a single Agent with LangGraph → add tool calls → add memory → try multi-agent collaboration.

Don't start with advanced frameworks. First hand-write a simple Agent with pure Python + OpenAI API (~100 lines of code) to understand what's happening at each step, then use frameworks to improve efficiency. This is the same principle as learning programming — write it by hand first, then use libraries.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: Model Context Protocol Official Documentation、Anthropic MCP Announcement
  • Prerequisites: Understanding of LLM API calls, basic understanding of Agent concepts
  • Programming language: Python / TypeScript
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 15+

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard protocol launched by Anthropic in late 2024, enabling AI models to connect to external tools and data sources in a unified way. Think of it as the USB interface for AI — previously each tool had to be individually adapted for each model; now as long as a tool supports MCP, any MCP-compatible AI can use it directly.

Core value of MCP: - Standardization: Implement once, use everywhere - Security: Clear permission boundaries — tools can only expose declared functionality - Ecosystem: Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, VS Code, and other mainstream tools already support MCP

In 2025, MCP has become the industry standard for AI tool integration. Understanding and using MCP is an essential skill for AI engineers.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Official documentation: modelcontextprotocol.io — The most authoritative learning resource
  • SDK: MCP Python SDK、MCP TypeScript SDK
  • Server collection: MCP Servers (GitHub Awesome) — Community-maintained MCP server list

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Understanding the MCP protocol:

  • MCP Official Docs - Overview — Architecture overview, required reading
  • MCP Specification — Protocol details
  • What is MCP? (Anthropic Blog) — Official announcement, understand the design motivation

MCP's three primitives:

  1. Tools: Functions the AI can invoke, such as search, computation, API calls
  2. Resources: Data the AI can read, such as files, database records
  3. Prompts: Predefined prompt templates for standardizing common tasks

Using existing MCP servers:

  • GitHub MCP Server — Let AI operate GitHub (PRs, Issues, code search)
  • Filesystem MCP Server — Let AI read/write local files
  • PostgreSQL MCP Server — Let AI query databases
  • Brave Search MCP Server — Let AI search the web
  • Slack MCP Server — Let AI operate Slack

Building your own MCP server:

  • MCP Python SDK Quickstart — Build an MCP server from scratch
  • MCP Inspector — Visual tool for debugging MCP servers
  • Building MCP Servers (Tutorial) — Official tutorial

MCP client integration:

  • Claude Desktop MCP Configuration — Using MCP in Claude Desktop
  • Cursor MCP Integration — Using MCP in Cursor
  • Using MCP tools in LangGraph — Integrating MCP tools into Agent workflows

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
MCP is still evolving rapidly, with the specification and SDKs changing frequently. When learning, rely on the official documentation as the source of truth — third-party tutorials may already be outdated.

Practical advice: first use existing MCP servers (like GitHub MCP) to get a feel for MCP's capabilities, then try building a simple MCP server with the Python SDK (e.g., wrapping your own project's API). Writing an MCP server takes roughly 50-100 lines of code — a great hands-on project.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: Stanford CS231n (Computer Vision), CLIP paper, HuggingFace multimodal course
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, basic understanding of neural network concepts
  • Programming language: Python
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 20+

"Modality" refers to the type of information: text is a modality, images are a modality, and so is audio. Multimodal models can understand and generate multiple types of information simultaneously — for example, GPT-4V can see images and describe them, GPT-4o can listen to audio and respond with voice, and Sora can generate videos from text.

For CS students, understanding multimodality isn't just about using APIs — it's about knowing the principles behind these capabilities: vision encoders, cross-modal alignment, unified generation architectures. This way, you can make the right technical choices in future projects.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Course videos: Stanford CS231n: Deep Learning for Computer Vision
  • Papers: CLIP、DALL-E 3
  • Interactive demos: HuggingFace Spaces — search "multimodal" for many interactive demos

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Computer vision fundamentals (understanding the image side):

  • Stanford CS231n — Fei-Fei Li's classic course, understand CNNs and visual representations
  • fast.ai Practical Deep Learning — More hands-on, train an image classification model in a few lines of code
  • HuggingFace Computer Vision Course — Free, covers ViT, object detection, image segmentation

Multimodal model principles (understanding cross-modal alignment):

  • CLIP paper explanation — OpenAI's image-text alignment model, the foundation of multimodality
  • Jay Alammar - The Illustrated CLIP — Intuitive visual explanation of CLIP
  • LLaVA: Large Language and Vision Assistant — A leading open-source multimodal model
  • Flamingo (DeepMind) — Pioneer in interleaved image-text sequence modeling

Hands-on practice:

  • Transformers Vision Tutorial — HuggingFace official vision task tutorials
  • Gradio — Quickly build web demo interfaces for multimodal models, just a few lines of code
  • OpenAI Vision API — The fastest way to get started with multimodal capabilities (if budget allows)

Frontier directions (good to know):

  • Sora — Current state-of-the-art in video generation
  • GPT-4o — Natively omnimodal (text/image/audio/video unified processing)
  • Gemini — Google's multimodal model family

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: You don't need to master every direction in multimodality. The minimum for CS students: understand how vision encoders (ViT) convert images to vectors, and understand how cross-modal alignment (CLIP) puts text and images in the same "semantic space." Once these two concepts are clear, other multimodal models are largely variations on the same theme, and reading papers won't feel overwhelming.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: Andrew Ng's DeepLearning.AI courses, Andrej Karpathy's "Neural Networks: Zero to Hero"
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, basic linear algebra and probability (matrix operations and conditional probability)
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 20+

Large Language Models (LLMs) are the foundation of today's AI technology. Whether you want to do prompt engineering, RAG, or Agents, understanding the fundamentals of LLMs is a necessary starting point. This course won't dive into paper-level mathematical derivations — instead, it focuses on building intuition: helping you understand why models "can talk," why they "make mistakes," and how to "train them."

Traditional NLP required training separate models for each task (translation, summarization, classification). The revolutionary aspect of LLMs is: one model handles all language tasks, and can even generalize to tasks it never saw during training. Three things power this: the Transformer architecture, large-scale pre-training, and human feedback alignment (RLHF).

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Course website: DeepLearning.AI Short Courses
  • Course videos: Andrej Karpathy - Neural Networks: Zero to Hero (YouTube)
  • Supplementary reading: Andrej Karpathy - The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Recurrent Neural Networks

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Must-watch for beginners (building intuition):

  • 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks — Visual explanations of neural networks, beginner-friendly
  • Andrej Karpathy - Neural Networks: Zero to Hero — Implement backpropagation and GPT from scratch, excellent free course
  • Andrew Ng - ChatGPT Prompt Engineering for Developers — 2-hour quick start, covers LLM usage and principles overview

Systematic study (deeper understanding):

  • Andrew Ng - Generative AI for Everyone — Coursera course, 16 hours, great for building a systematic understanding
  • HuggingFace NLP Course — Free, covers Tokenizer, model architecture, fine-tuning, and full pipeline
  • Stanford CS229 (Machine Learning) — Traditional ML fundamentals, understanding the "past life" of LLMs

Papers & explainers:

  • Attention Is All You Need — The original Transformer paper, worth reading at least the abstract and figures
  • Andrej Karpathy - State of GPT (2023) — The complete GPT training pipeline explained in one hour
  • Jay Alammar - The Illustrated Transformer — The most intuitive visual explanation of Transformers

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: If you're short on time, the most efficient path is: 3Blue1Brown Neural Networks series (2h) → Karpathy Zero to Hero first 3 lessons (4h) → Andrew Ng ChatGPT course (2h). About 8 hours total to build a solid intuition for how LLMs work. Don't start with papers — build intuition first, then go deeper progressively.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: HuggingFace official documentation and courses, ollama project
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, basic understanding of LLM concepts
  • Programming language: Python
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 15+

Without understanding the open-source model ecosystem, you can't truly build AI applications. This course teaches you how to find, evaluate, download, and use the right model for your needs — whether via cloud API or running locally.

The current open-source LLM ecosystem consists of several core components: HuggingFace Hub (model & dataset hosting platform, like GitHub for AI), open-source models (Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral, etc.), local inference tools (ollama, llama.cpp, vLLM), and model selection methodology (how to choose based on your hardware and task).

For CS students, understanding this ecosystem matters because: it keeps you from being locked into any single provider, and lets you flexibly choose solutions based on cost, privacy, latency, and other requirements.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Course: HuggingFace Course、ollama
  • Model leaderboards: HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard、Chatbot Arena (LMSYS)
  • Local inference tools: ollama、llama.cpp、vLLM

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Getting started with HuggingFace:

  • HuggingFace NLP Course — Free English course, covers Transformers library usage, model download, fine-tuning, deployment
  • HuggingFace Model Hub — Browse and search 100K+ open-source models
  • HuggingFace Tokenizers — Understand how text is "read" by models

Running models locally:

  • ollama — One command to run open-source models, supports Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral, and more. Best for getting started
  • llama.cpp — C++ local inference engine, can run quantized models on laptop CPUs, supports GGUF format
  • LM Studio — GUI for running models locally, great for those uncomfortable with the command line
  • vLLM — High-performance inference engine for high-throughput server-side deployment

Model selection guide:

  • HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard — Benchmark rankings for open-source models
  • Chatbot Arena — Human blind-test voting rankings, reflecting real-world experience
  • Can I run this model? — Check whether your hardware can run a given model

Chinese open-source models (2025 mainstream):

  • Qwen (Tongyi Qianwen) — Alibaba open-source, strong Chinese capabilities, full range (0.5B-72B)
  • DeepSeek — DeepSeek-V3/R1, performance approaching top closed-source models
  • GLM (Zhipu) — Tsinghua open-source, strong bilingual Chinese-English capabilities
  • MiniCPM — BAAI open-source, star among small on-device models

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Recommended path: install ollama and pull a 7B model first (get a feel for it) → browse HuggingFace Hub for interesting models → learn to write inference scripts with the Transformers library → pick a direction to go deeper (local optimization or cloud deployment) based on your needs. Don't try to understand all tools at once — start using them first.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: OWASP LLM Top 10、Lakera Gandalf、Anthropic AI Safety
  • Prerequisites: Basic understanding of LLM concepts and usage
  • Course difficulty: 🌟�🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 15+

As AI becomes increasingly embedded in production systems, security is no longer something you can bolt on later. From Prompt Injection attacks to data leaks, from model bias to toxic output — AI system security issues are unique and require specialized knowledge and tools.

For AI engineers, security capability is not optional — it's a core competency. An insecure AI system might: leak user privacy, be hijacked by attackers to perform malicious operations, produce harmful content leading to legal risk, or harm specific groups due to bias.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Guide: OWASP LLM Top 10 — The authoritative list of AI application security risks
  • Article: Anthropic - Challenges in Evaluating AI Systems — Anthropic's AI safety evaluation methodology
  • Article: Anthropic - Measuring Progress on Scalable Oversight

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Prompt Injection attack and defense:

  • OWASP LLM01: Prompt Injection — The most dangerous AI attack vector
  • Lakera Gandalf — Interactive Prompt Injection challenge game, from Level 1 to Level 7, intuitively understand attack principles
  • Simon Willison - Prompt Injection — The most comprehensive collection of Prompt Injection technical articles
  • PromptArmor — Tool for detecting Prompt Injection
  • Lakera Red — Enterprise-grade AI security protection

AI Alignment:

  • Anthropic - Core Views on AI Safety — Anthropic's perspective on AI safety
  • OpenAI Safety — OpenAI's safety research
  • Constitutional AI (Anthropic) — Method for constraining model behavior with rules
  • RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) — How models are "trained" via human feedback

AI bias and fairness:

  • Google PAIR - Machine Learning Fairness — Google's ML fairness guide
  • IBM AI Fairness 360 — Open-source AI bias detection toolkit
  • HuggingFace - Evaluating Bias — Practical guide to evaluating LLM bias

Responsible AI frameworks:

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework — NIST's AI risk management framework
  • EU AI Act — EU AI Act, understand AI regulatory trends
  • Google AI Principles — Google's AI principles
  • Microsoft Responsible AI — Microsoft's responsible AI practices

Data privacy:

  • OpenAI Data Usage Policy — Understand how data is used during API calls
  • Differential Privacy — Mathematical method for protecting training data privacy
  • Presidio (Microsoft) — Data anonymization and PII detection tool

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
You don't need to become a security expert, but you do need to build security awareness. Minimum requirements: 1. Understand Prompt Injection principles and defense methods 2. Know what sensitive data your AI system might leak 3. Understand basic AI bias detection methods 4. Know the contents of OWASP LLM Top 10

Lakera Gandalf game is the best starting point — spend 1-2 hours completing it, and you'll intuitively understand how attackers bypass AI safety guardrails.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: OpenAI Cookbook、Anthropic Building with the Claude API、Full Stack LLM Bootcamp
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, understanding of LLM API calls, basic web development knowledge
  • Programming language: Python
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 20+

Calling an LLM API to build a demo is easy, but making an LLM application production-ready is a completely different story. This course covers engineering practices for LLM application development: how to design reliable API call layers, handle streaming output, implement error handling and retries, control costs, and evaluate output quality.

These skills are the key leap from "toy project" to "real product." Andrew Ng repeatedly emphasized at Buildathon: the core capability of an AI engineer isn't tuning models — it's building reliable AI systems.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Documentation: OpenAI Cookbook — OpenAI's official code example collection covering various use cases
  • Documentation: Anthropic API Documentation — Claude API official docs with rich engineering practices
  • Course: Full Stack LLM Bootcamp (Stanford) — Stanford's free course covering full-stack LLM application development

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
API call engineering:

  • OpenAI Cookbook - API best practices — Streaming output, error handling, retry strategies, concurrent requests
  • Anthropic - Streaming Messages — Streaming output implementation guide
  • tenacity (Python retry library) — Add exponential backoff retries to API calls
  • LiteLLM — Unified API interface for 100+ LLM providers, avoid vendor lock-in

Cost management:

  • OpenAI Cookbook - Managing Costs — Rate limiting, budget control, token counting
  • tiktoken (OpenAI Token Counter) — Precisely calculate token usage before API calls
  • Token cost calculator — Unified token cost calculation across models

Output quality assurance:

  • Structured Output (OpenAI) — Enforce specific JSON format from model output
  • Instructor (Python) — Pydantic-based structured output library
  • Guardrails AI — Framework for validating and filtering LLM output
  • LMQL — Embed constraints and logic directly in prompts

Evaluation and monitoring:

  • LangSmith (LangChain) — Debugging, testing, and monitoring platform for LLM applications
  • Braintrust — LLM evaluation platform with automated testing support
  • OpenEvals (LangChain) — Open-source LLM evaluation framework
  • DeepEval — pytest-like testing framework for LLMs

Web framework integration:

  • FastAPI — The go-to Python framework for building LLM backend services
  • Streamlit — Quickly build front-end interfaces for LLM applications
  • Gradio — Quickly create interactive demos for ML/AI models
  • Next.js + Vercel AI SDK — Full-stack LLM application development

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Engineering capability is the key differentiator between "can use AI" and "AI engineer." Recommended path: first build a simple backend with FastAPI + OpenAI API → add streaming output → add error handling and retries → add cost monitoring → add evaluation. With each addition, your understanding of production-grade AI systems deepens.

Don't neglect evaluation — an LLM application without evaluation is like software without tests. You'll never know when it breaks in production.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: OpenEvals、RAGAS、DeepEval
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, understanding of LLM API calls
  • Programming language: Python
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 15+

Traditional software testing has clear pass/fail criteria, but LLM output is natural language — how do you judge whether it's right? This is what LLM evaluation solves.

LLM evaluation is the most underestimated aspect of AI engineering. Without evaluation, you cannot: - Know whether your prompt changes actually improved results - Detect whether your RAG system is retrieving wrong documents - Determine if the model is hallucinating in specific scenarios - Prove to your team/users that your AI system is trustworthy

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Documentation: RAGAS Documentation — Focused on RAG system evaluation
  • Documentation: DeepEval Documentation — pytest-like LLM testing framework
  • Paper: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) — Stanford's comprehensive LLM evaluation framework

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Evaluation methodology:

  • OpenEvals (LangChain) — Open-source evaluation framework with custom evaluator support
  • RAGAS — Specifically evaluates RAG system quality (faithfulness, relevance, precision)
  • DeepEval — Write LLM tests like pytest
  • Promptfoo — Command-line LLM evaluation tool, supports cross-model comparison

Evaluation dimensions:

Dimension Meaning Tool Faithfulness Whether the answer is based on retrieved documents, not fabricated RAGAS Relevance Whether retrieved documents are relevant to the question RAGAS Correctness Whether the answer is factually correct LLM-as-Judge Safety Whether it contains harmful content OpenAI Moderation API Fluency Whether the language is natural and fluent Human evaluation Latency Whether response time is acceptable Custom monitoring

LLM-as-Judge (using LLMs to evaluate LLMs):

  • OpenAI Cookbook - LLM-as-Judge — Use strong models to evaluate weaker models' output
  • JudgeLM — Purpose-built judge model
  • Note: LLM-as-Judge itself has biases and requires human calibration

Observability:

  • LangSmith — Tracing, debugging, and monitoring for LLM applications
  • LangFuse — Open-source LLM observability platform with tracing, evaluation, and prompt management
  • Weights & Biases (W&B) — ML experiment tracking, also supports LLM evaluation tracking
  • OpenTelemetry for LLM — Standardized telemetry data for LLM applications

Evaluation datasets:

  • MMLU — Massive Multitask Language Understanding benchmark
  • HumanEval — Code generation capability evaluation
  • MT-Bench — Multi-turn conversation capability evaluation
  • AlpacaEval — Instruction-following capability evaluation

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Evaluation is the foundation of iterative development. Without evaluation data, your improvements are "feels better" not "measurably better." Build the evaluation habit from the start: every time you change a prompt or switch models, run the evaluation suite and let data drive decisions.

Getting started: first use RAGAS to evaluate your RAG system (5 minutes to get started), then use DeepEval to write test cases for your LLM application. Together these two tools cover most evaluation needs.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: Comprehensive compilation
  • Prerequisites: Proficiency in at least one programming language (Python/Java/C++)
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 10+

AI-Assisted Programming refers to a set of methods and practices that leverage AI tools to improve software development efficiency. It's not about AI replacing you as a programmer — it's about AI becoming your programming partner: you think and decide, AI executes and accelerates.

From GitHub Copilot's code completion, to Claude Code's agentic programming, to Cursor's IDE integration — AI programming tools are evolving rapidly. But tools are just the means; the core is how you collaborate with AI.

docs.title: Why This Course
docs.text:
AI tools are reshaping how we program. Andrew Ng demonstrated at Buildathon how AI-assisted programming can accelerate independent developers' prototyping speed by 10x. This isn't the future — it's happening now.

Knowing how to use a tool doesn't mean using it well. Many people use ChatGPT to write code with mixed results. The difference: do you understand AI's capabilities and limitations? Do you know how to express requirements clearly? Can you review AI-generated code?

Avoid the Vibe Coding trap. Accepting AI-generated code without review looks fast in the short term but accumulates massive technical debt and security vulnerabilities over time. This course teaches the correct way to collaborate with AI.

docs.title: Evolution of AI Programming Tools
docs.text:
• Code completion era: Tabnine, GitHub Copilot — context-based line/function completion
  • Conversational programming era: ChatGPT, Claude — generate code through natural language conversation
  • Agentic programming era: Claude Code, Cursor, Devin — AI can autonomously plan and execute multi-step tasks
  • IDE integration era: AI deeply integrated into development environments, understanding entire codebases

docs.title: Core Collaboration Patterns
docs.text:
• You describe, AI generates: Describe requirements in natural language, AI produces code drafts
  • AI generates, you review: Carefully read AI-generated code, verify correctness
  • You write the framework, AI fills the details: You design architecture and interfaces, AI implements specific functions
  • AI-assisted debugging: Give error messages to AI for help locating and fixing issues
  • AI-assisted refactoring: Let AI improve code structure, naming, and performance

docs.title: Keys to Writing Good Prompts
docs.text:
• Provide clear context: Tell AI the language, framework, and coding standards of your project
  • Decompose complex tasks: Break large tasks into small ones, complete them incrementally
  • Provide examples: Use Few-Shot examples to help AI understand your desired style
  • Specify output format: Be explicit about function signatures, return values, error handling
  • Iterate and refine: If the first result isn't satisfactory, add more information and regenerate

docs.title: Code Review Checklist for AI-Generated Code
docs.text:
• Does the code handle edge cases correctly?
  • Are there security vulnerabilities (SQL injection, XSS, path traversal, etc.)?
  • Are there performance issues (unnecessary loops, memory leaks)?
  • Are names clear? Is the logic readable?
  • Is there test coverage?

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Pick one tool and use it deeply. Recommended starting point: Claude Code or Cursor — they represent the current state of the art in AI programming tools. Use them in real projects, not just tutorials.

Start with simple tasks. Have AI help you write utility functions, write tests, and refactor small code blocks. Gradually build accurate expectations of AI capabilities.

Always review. Every line of AI-generated code should pass through your review. This isn't about distrusting AI — it's about taking responsibility for your own code.

Combine with TDD practice. Write tests first, then have AI generate the implementation. Tests are the "contract" for AI-generated code and your safety net for verifying correctness.

docs.title: Recommended Resources
docs.text:
Tools

  • Claude Code — Anthropic's official agentic coding tool, available in terminal, IDE, and web
  • Cursor — AI-native IDE with deep codebase understanding
  • GitHub Copilot — The original AI code completion tool, integrated with VS Code
  • Windsurf — AI IDE from Codeium
  • Devin — Autonomous AI programming agent from Cognition

Courses and articles

  • Andrew Ng - AI Agents in LangGraph — Understand the underlying logic of agentic programming
  • Anthropic - Building Effective Agents — Best practices for Agent design
  • Simon Willison - AI Assisted Programming — Extensive practical experience sharing

Related courses

  • Claude Code Guide — Deep dive into Claude Code configuration and usage
  • Test-Driven Development — TDD is the best companion to AI-assisted programming

docs.title: What Is Claude Code
docs.text:
Claude Code is Anthropic's official agentic coding tool. It runs in your terminal, IDE, or browser, understands your entire codebase, and helps you complete programming tasks through natural language conversation.

The fundamental difference from tools like GitHub Copilot: Copilot is a completion tool that suggests code as you type; Claude Code is an agent that can read your project structure, execute shell commands, read and write files, run tests, manage Git workflows, and connect to external services.

Put simply, Copilot helps you write individual lines of code. Claude Code helps you complete entire tasks.

Core features:

  • Conversational collaboration: Describe what you need in natural language, Claude Code understands and executes
  • Codebase understanding: It reads your project before making changes
  • Autonomous execution: Runs commands, reads/writes files, handles errors without step-by-step instructions
  • Multi-platform support: Terminal CLI, VS Code extension, JetBrains plugin, Desktop app, Web (claude.ai/code)

docs.title: The Problem with Vibe Coding
docs.text: Before discussing Claude Code, it's worth understanding a popular but problematic approach to AI programming — Vibe Coding.

docs.title: What Is Vibe Coding
docs.text:
In February 2025, former Tesla AI Director and OpenAI co-founder Andrej Karpathy posted a tweet describing a programming approach he called "Vibe Coding":

"There's a new kind of coding I call 'vibe coding', where you fully give in to the vibes, embrace exponentials, and forget that the code even exists."

His workflow: use AI tools to generate code, accept all suggestions without reading diffs, copy-paste error messages to let AI fix them, and not worry that the code exceeds his own comprehension. He acknowledged this works "for throwaway weekend projects."

docs.title: The Drawbacks of Vibe Coding
docs.text:
Sounds appealing, but the data tells a different story.

Security vulnerabilities skyrocket

CodeRabbit analyzed 470 open-source GitHub pull requests in December 2025 and found that AI co-authored code contained 1.7x more major issues than human-written code, 2.74x more security vulnerabilities, and 75% more misconfigurations. Code written with Vibe Coding approaches has even worse security — because nobody reviews it.

Code quality drops sharply

GitClear published a longitudinal study in early 2025 analyzing 211 million lines of code changes from 2020-2024. Refactoring dropped from 25% to under 10% of changed lines, code duplication quadrupled, and code churn nearly doubled — all trends linked to AI-assisted coding. In September 2025, senior engineers began reporting "development hell" when maintaining AI-generated codebases — dubbed the "vibe coding hangover."

Productivity is an illusion

METR conducted a randomized controlled trial in July 2025. Experienced open-source developers were actually 19% slower when using AI coding tools. But here's the interesting part: before the study, they predicted they'd be 24% faster, and afterward they still believed they'd been 20% faster. This reveals a significant cognitive bias in how people perceive AI tool efficiency.

Real incidents

In July 2025, Replit's AI agent deleted a production database despite explicit instructions not to make any changes, then provided inaccurate information about what happened. The CEO publicly apologized.

Skill atrophy

Developers who rely on Vibe Coding long-term gradually lose their core programming skills. Not understanding your own code means: unable to debug independently, unable to make architectural decisions, unable to review others' code, unable to maintain your own projects. You transform from a "developer" into an "AI operator."

docs.title: Simon Willison's Distinction
docs.text:
Simon Willison (Django co-founder) drew an important boundary: if you carefully review AI-generated code, test it, and ensure you understand every line, that's not Vibe Coding — that's simply "using an LLM as a typing assistant."

The defining characteristic of Vibe Coding is giving up understanding and control. This is precisely what distinguishes it from AI-assisted programming.

docs.title: Human-AI Collaboration, Not Human-AI Replacement
docs.text:
The problem with Vibe Coding is that it turns developers into bystanders. The AI-assisted programming philosophy is the opposite: you are always the decision-maker, AI is the executor.

You are responsible for:

  • Defining requirements and acceptance criteria
  • Making architectural decisions
  • Judging code quality
  • Directing the project

Claude Code is responsible for:

  • Understanding the codebase
  • Executing concrete coding tasks
  • Running commands and tests
  • Suggesting approaches

This division keeps you in control of your code while dramatically improving efficiency.

docs.title: Understanding First, Generation Second
docs.text:
AI-assisted programming emphasizes understanding before acting, not blindly generating code. Good AI coding tools read relevant files first, understand existing architecture and patterns, then make changes consistent with your project's style.

This contrasts sharply with Vibe Coding's "generate first, ask questions later." Understanding-first means generated code is more likely to align with your existing codebase, reducing integration costs.

docs.title: Graduated Trust
docs.text:
Nobody suggests letting AI handle core business logic from day one. The right approach:

  1. Start with small tasks — write a test, fix a small bug, refactor a function
  2. Verify results — run tests, review diffs, read the code
  3. Expand gradually — once you have accurate expectations of AI capabilities, increase task scope
  4. Maintain verification — tests, code review, manual validation

This graduated approach lets you enjoy AI efficiency without losing control of your code.

Claude Code's design embodies all of the above principles. Let's look at how to install and use it.

docs.title: Installation Methods
docs.text:
Windows (recommended):

irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iex

macOS / Linux (recommended):

curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash

Homebrew (macOS / Linux):

brew install --cask claude-code

npm (still works but deprecated):

npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

IDE extensions:

  • VS Code: Search "Claude Code" in the Marketplace, or run code --install-extension anthropic.claude-code
  • JetBrains: Search "Claude Code" in JetBrains Marketplace

docs.title: First-Time Setup
docs.text:
After installation, verify and log in:

claude --version    # Confirm installation
claude auth login   # Log in to Anthropic account

Start your first session:

claude              # Enter interactive session

In a session, use /init to let Claude Code analyze your project and auto-generate a CLAUDE.md configuration file. This file records your project's architecture, build commands, coding standards, and other information to help Claude Code better understand your project.

Use /config to open the settings interface and adjust model, permission mode, and other options.

docs.title: Interactive Sessions
docs.text:
The most basic way to use Claude Code is interactive sessions. Run claude in your project directory to start talking with the AI:

# Start a new session
claude

# Start with an initial prompt
claude "Help me check this project's test coverage"

# Continue the most recent conversation
claude -c

# Resume a specific session
claude -r "abc123"

# Non-interactive mode (for scripts and CI)
claude -p "Run all tests and report results"

In a session, you can describe requirements like chatting with a colleague. Claude Code reads relevant files, executes commands, and modifies code — you can see what it's doing throughout and review at key checkpoints.

docs.title: Permission Modes
docs.text:
Claude Code has multiple permission modes that control whether it needs your approval before executing operations:

Mode Operations without approval Best for default Read-only Sensitive projects, first-time use acceptEdits Read/write files, common filesystem commands Daily development plan Read-only Exploring a codebase auto All operations (with safety classifier) Long tasks bypassPermissions All operations (no safety checks) Isolated containers/VMs only

Press Shift+Tab in the CLI to switch modes. For daily use, acceptEdits is recommended — maintain the habit of reviewing uncertain operations.

docs.title: Common Slash Commands
docs.text:
Claude Code provides rich slash commands, organized by workflow:

During a task:

  • /plan — Enter plan mode, plan before executing
  • /model — Switch models (e.g., from Sonnet to Opus for complex tasks)
  • /effort — Adjust effort level (low/medium/high/max)
  • /compact — Compress conversation context, free up token space
  • /context — View current context usage

Before committing code:

  • /diff — View uncommitted changes
  • /code-review — Review current diff, find bugs and improvements
  • /security-review — Analyze changes for security vulnerabilities

Session management:

  • /clear — Clear current conversation, start fresh
  • /resume — Resume a previous session
  • /branch — Branch current conversation

When something goes wrong:

  • /rewind — Roll back code and conversation to the last checkpoint
  • /doctor — Diagnose installation and configuration issues
  • /debug — Enable debug logging

Cost management:

  • /usage (or /cost) — View current session's token usage and costs

docs.title: CLAUDE.md Configuration System
docs.text:
CLAUDE.md is Claude Code's "project memory" file. It loads automatically at the start of every session, helping Claude Code understand your project.

File location hierarchy:

Scope Location Purpose User ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md Personal preferences for all projects Project ./CLAUDE.md or ./.claude/CLAUDE.md Team-shared project documentation Local ./CLAUDE.local.md Personal project config (should be in .gitignore)

Importing external files:

See @README.md for project overview
See @package.json for available npm commands

Rules directory:

Place modular, path-scoped instruction files in .claude/rules/:

---
paths:
  - "src/api/**/*.ts"
---
All API endpoints must include input validation.

Use /init to let Claude Code automatically analyze your project and generate an initial CLAUDE.md.

docs.title: MCP Server Integration
docs.text:
MCP (Model Context Protocol) lets Claude Code connect to external tools and services:

# Add GitHub MCP server
claude mcp add --transport http github https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ \
  --header "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_GITHUB_PAT"

# Add Sentry error tracking
claude mcp add --transport http sentry https://mcp.sentry.dev/mcp

# Add PostgreSQL database
claude mcp add --transport stdio db -- npx -y @bytebase/dbhub \
  --dsn "postgresql://readonly:pass@localhost:5432/mydb"

Management commands:

claude mcp list          # List all servers
claude mcp get <name>    # View details
claude mcp remove <name> # Remove server

Use /mcp in a session to check MCP server status and manage connections.

Project-level configuration is stored in .mcp.json, which can be committed to Git for team sharing.

docs.title: Hooks System
docs.text:
Hooks let you execute custom operations at Claude Code lifecycle events. For example, automatically running a linter after every file modification, or loading specific configurations at session start.

Common events:

  • PreToolUse — Triggered before tool execution (for interception and validation)
  • PostToolUse — Triggered after tool execution (for follow-up processing)
  • SessionStart — Triggered at session start

Configuration in .claude/settings.json:

{
  "hooks": {
    "PostToolUse": [
      {
        "matcher": "Write|Edit",
        "type": "command",
        "command": "ruff check --fix $CLAUDE_FILE_PATH"
      }
    ]
  }
}

docs.title: Scenario 1: Understanding an Existing Codebase
docs.text:
When taking over an unfamiliar project, Claude Code helps you build understanding quickly:

> What does this project do? Help me understand the overall architecture.

> How does the request handling flow work in the src/api/ directory?

> What's the call chain for this function? Which modules depend on it?

Claude Code reads relevant files, analyzes dependencies, and explains with clear structure. This is much faster than flipping through files one by one.

docs.title: Scenario 2: Implementing a New Feature
docs.text:
A complete collaboration from requirements to implementation:

> I want to add email verification to the user registration feature. First help me understand the current registration flow.

Claude Code reads the relevant code and understands the existing architecture. Then you continue:

> Good, now help me design the email verification approach, including database schema and API endpoints.

After reviewing the plan:

> The plan looks good, please implement it. Don't forget to write tests.

Key point: you made three decisions in this process — confirming requirements, reviewing the design, approving the implementation. Claude Code handled understanding the code, designing the solution, writing the implementation, and writing tests. You maintained control.

docs.title: Scenario 3: Fixing a Bug
docs.text:
> Users report that sessions are occasionally lost after login. The error log is at logs/error.log, help me locate the issue.

Claude Code reads the logs, analyzes the code, identifies the root cause, and proposes a fix. You can ask it to write a reproducing test first:

> Write a test that reproduces this issue first, then fix it.

Once the test passes, you have a verified fix and a regression test.

docs.title: Scenario 4: Code Refactoring
docs.text:
Refactoring is one of Claude Code's strongest scenarios because it requires understanding the overall code structure:

> The functions in src/utils/ are disorganized. Help me reorganize them by functionality without changing any external behavior.

Claude Code analyzes existing functions and their dependencies, proposes a refactoring plan, then executes under your review. If you have a test suite, running tests after refactoring confirms nothing is broken.

This echoes the TDD guide's point: tests are the safety net for refactoring.

docs.title: Scenario 5: Writing and Running Tests
docs.text:
> Write unit tests for src/services/payment.py, covering normal flows and edge cases.

Claude Code reads the source file, understands function inputs and outputs, generates test code, then runs it:

> Tests written, let me run them.

> 3 tests pass, 1 fails. The failing one is an edge case not handled properly, let me fix it.

This "generate-run-fix" cycle is Claude Code's natural workflow and embodies TDD principles in AI-assisted programming.

docs.title: AI-Assisted Programming vs Vibe Coding
docs.text:
Dimension Vibe Coding AI-Assisted Programming Code understanding None — "forget the code exists" Understand first, then modify Quality control No tests, no review Supports TDD, code review Security 2.74x vulnerability rate vs human code Built-in security review Learning effect Skill atrophy Skill growth Long-term value Technical debt accumulation Maintainable code Developer role Bystander Decision-maker

The core difference in one sentence: Vibe Coding gives up control; AI-assisted programming maintains control.

Developers using Vibe Coding may feel efficient in the short term, but long-term they're losing the ability to understand code, debug problems, and make architectural decisions. When AI-generated code breaks — and it will — they'll be helpless.

Developers using AI-assisted programming continuously improve: they learn to better express requirements, review code, design architecture, while delegating repetitive coding to AI. AI is an amplifier, not a replacement.

docs.title: Cost and Efficiency Management
docs.text:
Claude Code bills by token, so managing costs wisely matters.

Check spending:

/usage    # View current session's token usage and costs

Cost reduction strategies:

  • Choose the right model: Use Sonnet for daily tasks, reserve Opus for complex work. Switch with /model
  • Compress context: Use /compact when conversations get long to free up token space
  • Adjust effort level: Use /effort low for simple tasks to reduce unnecessary reasoning
  • Set budget caps: Use --max-budget-usd in non-interactive mode to limit spending
  • Keep CLAUDE.md lean: Stay under 200 lines to avoid loading excessive context every session
  • Clear unrelated conversations: Use /clear when starting new tasks

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Claude Code represents the right approach to AI-assisted programming: AI executes, you judge. It doesn't ask you to "forget the code exists" — it helps you understand and control code more efficiently.

For university students, learning to use tools like Claude Code isn't about mastering specific commands or configurations. It's about developing a collaborative mindset: knowing how to clearly express requirements, how to review generated code, how to balance efficiency with quality. These skills matter more in the AI era than ever before — because AI can write your code, but only you can decide what the code should do and whether it's doing it right.

docs.title: What is TDD
docs.text:
Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a development method where you write tests first, then write the implementation.

The traditional development flow looks like this:

  1. Write the functional code
  2. (If there's time) Add tests
  3. Hope the code is correct

TDD reverses this flow:

  1. First write a test that describes what you expect the code to do
  2. Write the minimum code to pass the test
  3. Refactor and optimize under the protection of tests

Note that TDD is not a testing technique — it's a development method. Tests are just the means; the real goal is to help you write better code.

docs.title: Red-Green-Refactor
docs.text:
The core TDD cycle has just three steps, repeated continuously:

Red — Write a failing test

You first define "what the code should do." This test must fail because you haven't written the implementation yet. Failure proves the test is working, not a fake test that always passes.

Green — Write the minimum code to pass the test

The goal isn't to write perfect code — it's to make the test pass. Even if the implementation is ugly or hardcoded, as long as the test passes, that's fine. The key word here is "minimum" — don't write more than you need.

Refactor — Optimize the code under the protection of tests

Now that the test passes, you can safely refactor: improve naming, eliminate duplication, optimize structure. If refactoring breaks anything, the tests will tell you immediately.

Then go back to Red, write a test for the next small feature, and repeat.

docs.title: Test-First Thinking
docs.text:
The most valuable aspect of TDD isn't "having tests" — it's thinking first about "how should the code be used."

When you write tests first, you're designing the API — what the function is called, what parameters it takes, what it returns. This forces you to think through the interface before writing the implementation, rather than writing code first and then backfilling a test.

Good interfaces are often designed from the consumer's (caller's) perspective, and tests are the earliest "consumer."

docs.title: Small Iterations
docs.text:
TDD requires handling only one small requirement at a time. Don't try to write everything at once — write one test, pass it, then write the next test. Each iteration is small, and the feedback loop is fast.

This lets you always know the state of your code: either all tests pass, or the step you just wrote has a problem. If something goes wrong, it's easy to locate because the changes are small.

docs.title: The Basic TDD Flow
docs.text:
Let's demonstrate with a simple example. Suppose we want to write a function that checks whether a number is prime.

Step 1: Write the test

# test_prime.py
from prime import is_prime

def test_1_is_not_prime():
    assert is_prime(1) is False

Run the test — it fails (Red). Because the is_prime function doesn't exist yet.

Step 2: Write the minimum implementation

# prime.py
def is_prime(n):
    return False

Run the test — it passes (Green). That's right, the simplest implementation is to just return False, since the only test case so far is input 1.

Step 3: Add more tests

def test_2_is_prime():
    assert is_prime(2) is True

Run the test — it fails (Red). Because now is_prime returns False for all inputs.

Step 4: Update the implementation

def is_prime(n):
    if n < 2:
        return False
    for i in range(2, n):
        if n % i == 0:
            return False
    return True

Run the test — all pass (Green).

Step 5: Refactor

The implementation logic is correct and the code is fairly clean, so no refactoring is needed for now.

Continue adding tests:

def test_4_is_not_prime():
    assert is_prime(4) is False

def test_7_is_prime():
    assert is_prime(7) is True

def test_9_is_not_prime():
    assert is_prime(9) is False

All pass. With each test you add, your confidence in the code grows.

docs.title: Why TDD Matters for AI-Assisted Programming
Challenges of AI-Generated Code
docs.text:
AI programming tools (like Claude, GitHub Copilot) can generate code quickly, but they also bring new problems:

  • "Looks right" doesn't mean it's right. AI-generated code is usually syntactically correct and logically smooth, but may fail under edge cases.
  • You may not fully understand the generated code. If you can't read the code, how can you be sure it's correct?
  • Without verification, confidence is low. Without tests, you can only manually try a few examples and hope nothing breaks in production.

The essence of these problems is: AI has dramatically reduced the cost of writing code, but hasn't reduced the cost of verifying code correctness.

docs.title: How TDD Solves These Problems
docs.text:
Tests as specifications. Before asking AI to generate code, you write the tests first — tests are your precise definition of requirements. With tests as context, AI is more likely to generate code that meets your expectations.

Automated verification. AI-generated code can be tested immediately. If it passes, the implementation meets the specification; if not, it needs adjustment. No need to review every line of AI code manually.

Safety net. When you need to modify or refactor AI-generated code, tests tell you if you've broken anything. This is especially important when doing secondary development on top of AI code.

Gradual trust. Through a set of tests, you can gradually build confidence in AI code. The more comprehensive the test coverage, the greater the confidence.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
In AI-assisted programming, the typical TDD workflow is:

  1. You describe the requirements in natural language
  2. AI generates the test code
  3. You review the tests — ensure they cover the correct scenarios
  4. AI generates the implementation code
  5. Tests automatically verify whether the implementation is correct
  6. You decide whether refactoring is needed

Key point: tests are the "contract" between humans and AI. You tell AI what you want through tests, and AI satisfies the tests with its implementation.

docs.title: Writing Good Prompts for AI
docs.text:
Including tests as part of your prompt produces much better results:

Please implement a Python function is_prime(n) that passes the following tests:

def test_1_is_not_prime():
    assert is_prime(1) is False

def test_2_is_prime():
    assert is_prime(2) is True

def test_4_is_not_prime():
    assert is_prime(4) is False

Compared to just saying "write a function to check if a number is prime," providing tests helps AI understand your requirements more accurately.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
Test quality still needs human oversight. AI may generate tests that "pass but have logic errors" — for example, the test itself has a bug, or the test coverage isn't comprehensive enough. You can't hand off tests entirely to AI and forget about them.

Edge cases require human initiative. AI won't automatically think about "what if the input is negative?" or "what if the input is 0?" These edge cases need you to proactively add tests.

Don't do TDD for TDD's sake. If it's a simple script or one-off data processing, strict TDD isn't necessary. TDD is best suited for code that needs long-term maintenance and has clear inputs and outputs.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
TDD is more valuable in the AI era than ever before. AI has made writing code cheap, but verifying code correctness remains the developer's core responsibility. TDD provides a systematic approach: using deterministic tests to tame the uncertainty of AI-generated code.

For university students, learning TDD isn't just about learning a development method — it's about cultivating the programming habit of "thinking clearly before acting." This habit is especially important in the age of AI-assisted programming — because AI can write code for you, but only you can decide what the code should do.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: LangChain RAG Course、LlamaIndex Documentation、Harrison Chase — Building Advanced RAG
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, basic understanding of LLM concepts, basic understanding of vector databases
  • Programming language: Python
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 25+

RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is currently the most mainstream LLM application technique. The core idea is simple: don't stuff all knowledge into the prompt — instead, "retrieve" relevant knowledge snippets when needed, then let the model generate based on that knowledge.

This addresses several fundamental LLM problems: - Hallucinations: Let the model answer based on retrieved real documents, not fabrication - Knowledge freshness: Training data has a cutoff date; RAG can connect to the latest external data - Domain knowledge: No fine-tuning needed to make the model "understand" your domain - Traceability: Answers can cite sources for user verification

Many "knowledge base Q&A" products you use today — customer service bots, document assistants, enterprise search — are essentially RAG under the hood. In Andrew Ng's Buildathon demo, RAG was the most frequently appearing building block.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Course: LangChain RAG Course (LangChain Academy) — Free, covers the complete RAG pipeline, highly recommended
  • Documentation: LlamaIndex Documentation — Another mainstream RAG framework with excellent documentation
  • Course videos: Andrew Ng - LangChain: Chat With Your Data — Short course, quick to get started

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Beginner (understanding the full RAG pipeline):

  • Andrew Ng - LangChain for LLM Application Development — 3 hours, covers RAG basics (chunking, embedding, retrieval, generation)
  • Jay Alammar - RAG from Scratch — Build a RAG system from scratch, understand what each component does
  • Full Stack LLM Bootcamp - RAG Lecture — Stanford's free LLM bootcamp RAG section, excellent quality

Advanced (engineering-grade RAG):

  • LangChain RAG from Scratch (YouTube Series) — Harrison Chase walks you through building RAG from scratch, covering advanced retrieval strategies
  • LlamaIndex - Advanced RAG Techniques — Covers HyDE, RAPTOR, Self-RAG, and other advanced techniques
  • RAG Survey Paper — Survey paper covering the academic frontier

Deep dive into retrieval quality (RAG bottlenecks are usually in retrieval, not generation):

  • ColBERT: Efficient and Effective Passage Search — Classic fine-grained retrieval method
  • HyDE: Hypothetical Document Embeddings — Use model-generated "hypothetical answers" to improve retrieval
  • Parent-Child Chunking Strategy — Practical technique for balancing retrieval granularity
  • Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts — Research showing models tend to lose information in the middle of long contexts, important for RAG chunk design

Hands-on project:

Build a "personal knowledge base Q&A system" from scratch: 1. Pick a domain you're familiar with (course notes, technical docs, novel setting bibles) 2. Use LangChain or LlamaIndex for document loading and chunking 3. Choose an Embedding model (recommend text-embedding-ada-002 or open-source bge-large-zh) 4. Store in a vector database (recommend Qdrant or Chroma) 5. Connect to an LLM for Q&A 6. Evaluate results, iterate on retrieval strategy

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: The hard part of RAG isn't "stuffing documents in" — it's retrieval quality. 90% of RAG effectiveness problems come from chunking strategy, Embedding selection, or retrieval algorithms. Start by building the simplest possible RAG pipeline (can be done in ~50 lines of code), then spend time evaluating and improving retrieval quality. Both LangChain and LlamaIndex are great for learning, but production environments usually require custom components.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
• Source: Qdrant、Chroma、Pinecone
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, understanding of Embedding concepts
  • Programming language: Python
  • Course difficulty: 🌟🌟
  • Estimated study hours: 10+

Vector databases are the "memory" component of RAG systems. Traditional databases store structured data (rows and columns); vector databases store vectors (Embeddings) — numerical semantic representations of text, images, and other content. Their core capability: given a query, quickly find the most semantically similar vectors.

This capability is called "Approximate Nearest Neighbor search" (ANN) — the underlying engine that lets RAG systems quickly retrieve relevant knowledge. Without a vector database, RAG would need to brute-force scan all documents — completely impractical at scale.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Documentation: Qdrant Quick Start
  • Documentation: Chroma Getting Started
  • Tutorial: Pinecone Learning Center

docs.title: Resource Summary
docs.text:
Comparison of major vector databases:

Database Open Source Managed Service Characteristics Best For Chroma ✅ ✅ Pure Python, zero config, in-memory/persistent Local development, prototyping Qdrant ✅ ✅ Rust implementation, high performance, supports filtered + vector hybrid search Production environments Milvus ✅ ✅ Distributed architecture, billion-scale vectors Large-scale vector search Weaviate ✅ ✅ GraphQL API, rich built-in modules Flexible query requirements Pinecone ❌ ✅ Fully managed, zero ops Fast time to market, no ops overhead pgvector ✅ ✅ PostgreSQL extension Already using PG, don't want new components

Hands-on tutorials:

  • Qdrant Python Client Tutorial — From zero to running in 15 minutes
  • Chroma Quickstart — Code is documentation, up and running in a few steps
  • LangChain + Qdrant Integration — Using Qdrant in a RAG pipeline

Embedding model selection:

  • OpenAI Embedding — text-embedding-3-small offers the best value
  • HuggingFace Massive Text Embedding Benchmark (MTEB) — Chinese Embedding model leaderboard
  • FlagOpen (BAAI) — Recommended open-source Embedding model for Chinese
  • Jina AI Embeddings — Supports 100+ languages, long-text Embedding

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Recommended path: Chroma (local development) → Qdrant (production deployment). Chroma requires no services to install — just pip install and go, perfect for learning. Qdrant single-node edition starts with one Docker command, feature-complete and a solid production choice.

pgvector is also worth watching — if your project already uses PostgreSQL, adding one extension gives you vector search without introducing any new components.

Three key concepts to understand: Embedding (text → vector), ANN search (quickly finding similar vectors), and metadata filtering (vector similarity + structured condition hybrid queries).

docs.title: What is a Distributed System
docs.text: Distributed systems study how multiple computers work together to accomplish tasks that a single computer cannot. Virtually every large-scale internet service — search engines, social networks, e-commerce, cloud storage — is powered by distributed systems.

docs.title: Why Learn Distributed Systems
docs.text:
Required for large-scale systems. Modern AI training requires thousands of GPUs working in coordination; AI inference needs deployment across global data centers.

Understand modern software architecture. Microservices, Kubernetes, message queues, distributed databases — all built on distributed systems theory.

Advanced interview topic. System Design Interviews are fundamentally about distributed system design.

docs.title: Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Definition: resource sharing, concurrency, no global clock, independent failures
  • Architecture: client-server, P2P, microservices

docs.title: Time and Clocks
docs.text: • Clock synchronization, logical clocks (Lamport, vector clocks)

docs.title: Replication and Consistency
docs.text: • Replication strategies, consistency models, CAP theorem, BASE theory

docs.title: Consensus Algorithms
docs.text: • Paxos, Raft, ZAB, Two-Phase Commit

docs.title: Distributed Data Storage
docs.text: • Sharding, distributed databases, distributed file systems

docs.title: Distributed Computing
docs.text: • MapReduce, Spark, distributed training

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Read classic papers first. Start with Google's "big three" (GFS, MapReduce, Bigtable).

Do the MIT 6.824 labs. Implement Raft and a Raft-based KV store.

Build a distributed cluster with Docker Compose. Deploy a Redis Cluster or Kafka cluster.

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description MIT 6.824 Course MIT distributed systems, implement Raft DDIA Distributed Chapters Textbook Designing Data-Intensive Applications - distributed chapters Google's Big Three Papers Original GFS, MapReduce, Bigtable papers

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Martin Kleppmann Designing Data-Intensive Applications
  • Prerequisites: Database fundamentals, networking fundamentals
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 40+

DDIA Part 2 "Distributed Data" is the most valuable section of the book and one of the best reading materials for getting started with distributed systems.

docs.title: Chapters 5-6: Replication
docs.text:
• Primary-replica, multi-leader, leaderless replication
  • Handling replication lag

docs.title: Chapters 7-8: Partitioning
docs.text:
• Hash partitioning, range partitioning
  • Secondary index partitioning
  • Partition rebalancing

docs.title: Chapter 9: Distributed Transactions and Consensus
docs.text:
• Two-Phase Commit (2PC)
  • Three-Phase Commit (3PC)
  • Paxos, Raft
  • Linearizability

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Read Chapters 5-6 (Replication) first. Replication is the most fundamental concept in distributed systems.

Chapter 9 is the highlight. Distributed transactions and consensus algorithms are interview favorites.

Pair with MIT 6.824. DDIA covers theory; 6.824 provides hands-on labs.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: DDIA's distributed section is more accessible than most distributed systems textbooks. If you find textbooks too theoretical, start with DDIA to build intuition.

docs.title: Google's Big Three: GFS, MapReduce, Bigtable
Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Google (Sanjay Ghemawat, Jeff Dean, et al.)
  • Prerequisites: OS fundamentals, networking fundamentals
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 20+

Google's three classic papers — GFS, MapReduce, Bigtable — laid the foundation for modern distributed systems. Hadoop, HBase, Spark, and other open-source projects were directly influenced by these papers.

Reading these papers, you'll understand core distributed systems design principles and see how industry solves real distributed problems.

docs.title: GFS: The Google File System
docs.text:
• Paper: GFS Paper (SOSP 2003)
  • Core idea: Distributed file system with data sharding and replication for high availability

docs.title: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
docs.text:
• Paper: MapReduce Paper (OSDI 2004)
  • Core idea: Programming model for large-scale data processing with automatic parallelization and fault tolerance

docs.title: Bigtable: A Distributed Storage System for Structured Data
docs.text:
• Paper: Bigtable Paper (OSDI 2006)
  • Core idea: Distributed structured data storage built on GFS and SSTable

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Read in order. GFS → MapReduce → Bigtable; the latter builds on the former.

Focus on design trade-offs. Each paper makes many engineering trade-offs. Understanding these trade-offs matters more than memorizing design details.

Pair with MIT 6.824. Papers cover design principles; 6.824 gives you hands-on implementation.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
These three papers are the "bible" of distributed systems. Even if you have time for no other distributed systems papers, these three are essential.

Later open-source projects (Hadoop, HBase, Cassandra, Spark) were directly influenced by these papers.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: MIT
  • Prerequisites: OS fundamentals, networking fundamentals, Go basics
  • Programming Language: Go
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Website: pdos.csail.mit.edu/6.824

MIT 6.824 is MIT's distributed systems course, widely regarded as the best in the world. The core of the course is implementing the Raft consensus algorithm and a Raft-based distributed KV store.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: pdos.csail.mit.edu/6.824
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Lab code: MIT 6.824 Labs

docs.title: Lab List
docs.text: Lab Content Difficulty Lab 1: MapReduce Implement MapReduce framework 🌟🌟 Lab 2: Raft Implement Raft consensus algorithm 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 3: KV Server Implement Raft-based KV store 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 4: Sharded KV Implement sharded KV store 🌟🌟🌟🌟🌟

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Solid Go skills required. The course uses Go; familiarity with goroutines and channels is essential.

Read the Raft paper first. The Raft paper is the course's core. Read it before starting labs.

Allocate 2-3 weeks per lab. The Raft lab is especially time-consuming; don't rush.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
6.824 has the best lab design in distributed systems courses. After completing all labs, your understanding will surpass most practitioners.

Raft is one of the most widely used consensus algorithms in industry (etcd, TiKV, CockroachDB all use Raft). Mastering it is a fundamental skill for distributed systems engineers.

docs.title: Why use Docker
docs.text:
While studying CS courses, you often encounter these problems:

  • Course labs require specific operating systems or software versions
  • Your local environment differs from the instructor's, causing different results
  • Installing software requires configuring many dependencies — tedious and error-prone

Docker solves these problems through containerization. Containers are like lightweight virtual machines that package your program with all its dependencies, ensuring it runs the same way everywhere.

Environment consistency, build once run anywhere

Docker containers encapsulate the complete runtime environment, including the OS, libraries, and dependencies. Code that runs locally will also run on servers and other people's machines. Say goodbye to "it works on my machine."

Fast deployment, instant startup

Compared to traditional virtual machines that take minutes to boot, Docker containers start in seconds and use fewer resources.

Essential for CS course labs

Many CS courses (operating systems, databases, networking) provide Docker images. Just pull and use — no more tedious environment setup.

docs.title: Installing Docker
docs.text:
Windows

Recommended: Docker Desktop for Windows.

Note: Docker Desktop requires WSL2 or Hyper-V. If you use WSL2 (recommended), Docker will automatically leverage the WSL2 Linux kernel.

macOS

Download Docker Desktop for Mac.

Linux

# Ubuntu
sudo apt update
sudo apt install docker.io
sudo systemctl start docker
sudo systemctl enable docker
# Add current user to docker group (avoid sudo)
sudo usermod -aG docker $USER

docs.title: Basic usage
docs.text:
Pull and run images

# Pull an Ubuntu image
docker pull ubuntu

# Run a container
docker run -it ubuntu bash

# Run a Python container
docker run -it python:3.11 bash

Common commands

docker ps                        # View running containers
docker ps -a                     # View all containers (including stopped)
docker stop <container_id>       # Stop a container
docker rm <container_id>         # Remove a container
docker images                    # View local images
docker rmi <image_id>            # Remove an image

Using Dockerfile

A Dockerfile is a text file that describes how to build an image:

FROM python:3.11
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
CMD ["python", "main.py"]

docker build -t myapp .
docker run -it myapp

Mounting local directories

Mount your local code directory into the container for easy development and debugging:

docker run -v $(pwd):/app -w /app -it python:3.11 bash

docs.title: Docker Compose
docs.text:
When your application needs multiple services (e.g., web server + database), Docker Compose lets you start and manage multiple containers at once:

# docker-compose.yml
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: example

docker compose up -d      # Start all services
docker compose down       # Stop all services

docs.title: Other resources
docs.text:
• Docker Official Documentation
  • Docker — From Beginners to Pros
  • Play with Docker — Try Docker online

docs.title: Why use Git
docs.text:
As you learn computer science, you'll constantly be writing code, working on projects, and collaborating with others. Without version control, you'll likely encounter situations like:

  • Breaking your code with no way to restore it
  • Files being overwritten during collaboration
  • Forgetting when and why a feature was added

Git is the world's most popular distributed version control system. Nearly all open-source projects and enterprise development rely on it. Mastering Git is not only essential for development but also a gateway to open-source communities and job opportunities.

Version control, never lose work
