xv6 is the "minimum viable system" for learning OS implementation. After reading xv6's code, you'll have an inside-out understanding of operating systems.

Since 2023, xv6 has migrated from x86 to RISC-V with cleaner code. The RISC-V version is recommended.

docs.title: Why CS Students Need Math
docs.text: Mathematics is the foundation of computer science. Algorithms, machine learning, computer graphics, cryptography — all require solid mathematical foundations. The AI era demands even more mathematical understanding than before.

docs.title: Calculus
docs.text: The foundation of understanding change and optimization. Gradient descent, backpropagation, probability density functions — all built on calculus.

docs.title: Linear Algebra
docs.text: The "language" of AI. Matrix operations, eigenvalues, SVD — the core operations of deep learning, computer vision, and NLP.

docs.title: Probability and Statistics
docs.text: The foundation of understanding uncertainty. Bayesian inference, maximum likelihood estimation, hypothesis testing — the theoretical framework of machine learning.

docs.title: Discrete Mathematics
docs.text: The formal language of computer science. Logic, set theory, graph theory, combinatorics — foundations of algorithm analysis, data structures, and cryptography.

docs.title: Learning Path
docs.text:
Freshman Fall: Calculus I + Linear Algebra Freshman Spring: Calculus II + Discrete Math Sophomore Fall: Probability and Statistics

If time is limited, priority: Linear Algebra > Calculus > Probability > Discrete Math.

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description 3Blue1Brown Series Video Visual explanations of linear algebra and calculus MIT 18.01/18.02 Calculus Course MIT calculus courses MIT 18.06 Linear Algebra Course MIT linear algebra, taught by Gilbert Strang MIT 6.042 Discrete Math Course MIT discrete math, CS perspective MIT 6.041 Probability Course MIT probability and statistics Khan Academy Math Platform Free, systematic math courses Paul's Online Math Notes Notes Calculus and linear algebra notes Mathematics for Machine Learning Book Math textbook designed for ML

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Khan Academy (Salman Khan)
  • Prerequisites: None, beginner-friendly
  • Difficulty: 🌟 ~ 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: Varies

Khan Academy is the world's largest free online education platform, founded by Salman Khan. Its math courses cover everything from elementary to university level, completely free.

Khan Academy's hallmark: short videos + extensive exercises + instant feedback. Each video is 5-15 minutes, covering one concept, followed by practice problems.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Website: khanacademy.org
  • Math courses: Khan Academy Math
  • Chinese version: Khan Academy CN (partial Chinese translation)
  • App: Official apps for iOS and Android

docs.title: Calculus Path
docs.text:
1. AP Calculus AB → Equivalent to Calculus I
  2. AP Calculus BC → Equivalent to Calculus II
  3. Multivariable Calculus

docs.title: Linear Algebra Path
docs.text:
1. Linear Algebra (foundations)
  2. Matrix Transformations

docs.title: Probability Path
docs.text:
1. Statistics and Probability (foundations)
  2. AP Statistics

docs.title: Discrete Math Path
docs.text:
1. Combinatorics
  2. Cryptography (involves number theory)

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Use Khan Academy for pre-class preview and post-class review. Its short videos are great for quick concept building.

Do the exercises. Khan Academy's exercise system provides instant feedback.

Use alongside formal courses. Khan Academy is best as a supplement, not a sole learning resource.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Khan Academy is completely free with no ads. It operates on donations and volunteer translations.

If your English is limited, use the Chinese version (zh.khanacademy.org), though it has less comprehensive coverage than the English version.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: MIT
  • Prerequisites: High school math (functions, trigonometry, exponentials)
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

MIT 18.01 (Single Variable Calculus) and 18.02 (Multivariable Calculus) are required math courses for science and engineering students at MIT.

Calculus is the foundation of understanding change and optimization. In AI, gradient descent, backpropagation, and probability density functions all build on calculus.

docs.title: MIT 18.01: Single Variable Calculus
docs.text:
• Website: ocw.mit.edu/18.01
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Textbook: Calculus with Analytic Geometry (Thomas & Finney)

docs.title: MIT 18.02: Multivariable Calculus
docs.text:
• Website: ocw.mit.edu/18.02
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Textbook: Multivariable Calculus (James Stewart)

docs.title: 18.01: Single Variable Calculus
docs.text:
• Limits and continuity
  • Derivatives and differentiation
  • Mean value theorem
  • Integration and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
  • Techniques of integration
  • Infinite series

docs.title: 18.02: Multivariable Calculus
docs.text:
• Vectors and analytic geometry
  • Partial derivatives and total differential
  • Directional derivatives and gradients
  • Multiple integrals
  • Line and surface integrals
  • Green's, Stokes', and Divergence theorems

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Study single variable before multivariable. Concepts like gradients and directional derivatives from multivariable calculus are heavily used in AI.

Pair with 3Blue1Brown's Essence of Calculus.

Focus on derivatives, integrals, gradients, and the chain rule. These are the most used calculus knowledge in AI and ML.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
If time is limited, complete at least 18.01 (single variable calculus), since derivatives and integrals are fundamental to optimization algorithms like gradient descent.

Topics from 18.02 (multivariable calculus) such as gradients, directional derivatives, and Lagrange multipliers are used in machine learning optimization theory.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: MIT
  • Prerequisites: High school math
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

MIT 6.042J is MIT's discrete mathematics course designed specifically for CS students. Covers logic, proof techniques, set theory, graph theory, combinatorics, and probability foundations.

Discrete mathematics is the formal language of computer science. Algorithm analysis, data structures, cryptography, compiler design — all depend on discrete math.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: ocw.mit.edu/6.042J
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Textbook: Mathematics for Computer Science (free PDF course notes)

docs.title: Part 1: Mathematical Foundations
docs.text:
• Propositional logic and proof techniques
  • Set theory and functions
  • Relations and partial orders

docs.title: Part 2: Graph Theory
docs.text:
• Basic graph concepts
  • Trees and spanning trees
  • Eulerian and Hamiltonian graphs
  • Graph coloring

docs.title: Part 3: Combinatorics
docs.text:
• Counting principles
  • Permutations and combinations
  • Inclusion-exclusion
  • Generating functions

docs.title: Part 4: Probability Foundations
docs.text:
• Conditional probability and independence
  • Random variables and expectation
  • Law of large numbers

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Focus on proof techniques. Mathematical induction, proof by contradiction, constructive proofs — these are used everywhere in algorithm analysis.

Graph theory is essential. It's the foundation of data structures, network algorithms, and social network analysis.

Do lots of exercises. The core of learning discrete math is solving problems, not just watching videos.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The MIT 6.042J course notes (free PDF) are excellent for self-study. The video lectures by Tom Leighton and Albert Meyer are also high quality.

If you're pursuing AI research, the probability foundations section is especially important.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: MIT (Professor Gilbert Strang)
  • Prerequisites: High school math
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

MIT 18.06 is MIT's linear algebra course and the most popular linear algebra course in the world. Professor Gilbert Strang is a legend in the field, known for intuitive explanations emphasizing understanding over rote computation.

The course's core philosophy: linear algebra is not about matrix computation — it's about understanding linear spaces and linear transformations.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: ocw.mit.edu/18.06
  • Lectures: YouTube (MIT OCW, free)
  • Textbook: Introduction to Linear Algebra (Gilbert Strang, 5th Edition)

docs.title: Part 1: Vectors and Matrices
docs.text:
• Vectors, matrices, linear systems
  • Elimination, matrix operations
  • Vector spaces, column space, null space

docs.title: Part 2: Orthogonality and Projections
docs.text:
• Dot product, orthogonality
  • Projections, least squares
  • Orthogonal bases, Gram-Schmidt

docs.title: Part 3: Determinants and Eigenvalues
docs.text:
• Determinant properties and computation
  • Eigenvalues and eigenvectors
  • Diagonalization

docs.title: Part 4: Applications
docs.text:
• Differential equations
  • Fourier series
  • Markov matrices
  • Linear programming

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Watch 3Blue1Brown's Essence of Linear Algebra first, then take this course. 3Blue1Brown builds intuition; Gilbert Strang builds the rigorous mathematical framework. Together, they're perfect.

Do the homework problems. Gilbert's problems are exceptionally well-designed.

Focus on Chapters 1-5 and 6-7. These topics (vector spaces, orthogonality, eigenvalues) are most used in AI and ML.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Gilbert Strang is over 80 years old, but his lectures remain clear and insightful. Many AI engineers say they gain new insights every time they revisit 18.06.

For advanced study, consider MIT 18.065 (Matrix Methods in Data Analysis, Signal Processing, and Machine Learning), designed specifically for AI-oriented students.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: MIT
  • Prerequisites: Calculus (18.01 level)
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

MIT 6.041 is MIT's probability theory and statistics course. Probability theory is the theoretical framework of machine learning — Bayesian inference, maximum likelihood estimation, hypothesis testing are all core AI tools.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: ocw.mit.edu/6.041
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Textbook: Introduction to Probability (Bertsekas & Tsitsiklis)

docs.title: Part 1: Probability Foundations
docs.text:
• Sample spaces and events
  • Conditional probability and independence
  • Bayes' theorem

docs.title: Part 2: Random Variables
docs.text:
• Discrete random variables (Bernoulli, Binomial, Poisson, Geometric)
  • Continuous random variables (Uniform, Normal, Exponential)
  • Expectation and variance
  • Joint and conditional distributions

docs.title: Part 3: Limit Theorems
docs.text:
• Law of large numbers
  • Central limit theorem

docs.title: Part 4: Statistical Inference
docs.text:
• Maximum likelihood estimation
  • Bayesian inference
  • Hypothesis testing
  • Regression analysis

docs.title: Part 5: Stochastic Processes (Advanced)
docs.text:
• Markov chains
  • Poisson processes

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Learn calculus before probability. Probability theory requires integration skills from calculus.

Focus on Bayes' theorem and maximum likelihood estimation. These concepts appear everywhere in machine learning.

Do lots of probability problems. The core of learning probability is building intuition, not memorizing formulas.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
MIT 6.041 is one of the best introductory probability courses. Professor John Tsitsiklis is a top researcher in probability theory.

If you're pursuing deep learning research, consider studying stochastic processes (MIT 6.262) — Markov chains are crucial in reinforcement learning.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Paul Dawkins (Lamar University)
  • Prerequisites: High school math
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟 ~ 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: Varies

Paul's Online Math Notes is a free math notes series written by Professor Paul Dawkins of Lamar University. It is one of the best free notes for university-level calculus and linear algebra.

The notes are known for being detailed, clear, with extensive worked examples. Unlike textbooks that emphasize axiomatic derivations, Paul's notes focus on "how to compute" and "how to apply."

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Calculus I: Calculus I Notes
  • Calculus II: Calculus II Notes
  • Calculus III: Calculus III Notes
  • Linear Algebra: Algebra Notes

docs.title: Calculus I
docs.text:
• Limits and continuity
  • Derivatives and differentiation
  • Applications of derivatives
  • Integration
  • Applications of integration

docs.title: Calculus II
docs.text:
• Techniques of integration
  • Parametric equations and polar coordinates
  • Infinite series
  • Taylor series

docs.title: Calculus III
docs.text:
• Vectors and analytic geometry
  • Partial derivatives
  • Multiple integrals
  • Line and surface integrals

docs.title: Linear Algebra
docs.text:
• Linear systems and matrices
  • Determinants
  • Vector spaces
  • Eigenvalues and eigenvectors

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Use alongside formal courses. Paul's notes are best for review after watching formal lecture videos.

Focus on the worked examples. The notes' greatest value is the extensive collection of detailed examples.

Do the practice problems. Each chapter has practice problems with solutions.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Paul's Online Math Notes is recommended by many students in China for graduate entrance exam preparation. The examples are moderately difficult and great for exam prep.

Note: The URLs may change over time. If the links don't work, search "Paul's Online Math Notes" on Google.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Marc Peter Deisenroth, A. Aldo Faisal, Cheng Soon Ong (Imperial College London)
  • Prerequisites: High school math
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

Mathematics for Machine Learning is a math textbook written specifically for machine learning. It covers all the mathematical foundations needed for ML: linear algebra, calculus, probability, and optimization theory.

The book's greatest strength: all mathematical content is framed around ML applications. You won't learn "useless" math — every concept has a corresponding application in ML.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official site: mml-book.github.io
  • Free PDF: PDF Download
  • GitHub: mml-book/mml-book.github.io

docs.title: Part 1: Mathematical Foundations
docs.text:
• Linear algebra (vectors, matrices, linear transformations, eigenvalues)
  • Analytic geometry (distance, projection, hyperplanes)
  • Matrix decompositions (SVD, eigendecomposition, Cholesky)

docs.title: Part 2: Mathematics for Machine Learning
docs.text:
• Vector calculus (gradients, directional derivatives, Hessian)
  • Probability and distributions (Bayesian inference, MLE)
  • Optimization theory (gradient descent, convex optimization, constrained optimization)

docs.title: Part 3: Applications
docs.text:
• Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
  • Gaussian Mixture Models
  • Linear Regression
  • Support Vector Machines (SVM)

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Learn linear algebra and calculus basics first, then read this book. The book assumes familiarity with basic linear algebra and calculus concepts.

Focus on Parts 1 and 2. These are the core mathematical foundations for machine learning.

Do the exercises. Each chapter has problems to test your understanding.

Use alongside other courses. This book works well as a supplement to MIT 18.06 (Linear Algebra) and MIT 18.01 (Calculus).

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The book is completely free, with PDF available on the official website. It is the companion textbook for Imperial College London's machine learning course.

If you only want to read one math book to prepare for machine learning, this is the best choice. It's more focused than traditional math textbooks and more mathematically rigorous than pure ML tutorials.

docs.title: What is a Database System
docs.text: A database system studies how to efficiently, reliably, and securely store and retrieve large amounts of data. Modern database systems solve core problems: fast lookups (indexing), concurrent operations (transactions and locks), and crash recovery (durability).

docs.title: Why Learn Database Systems
docs.text:
Foundation of backend development. Data access is inescapable in web development, microservices, and AI applications.

Understand data storage. Vector databases are essentially databases with vector search instead of B+ trees.

Interview essentials. Transaction ACID, indexing, SQL optimization — guaranteed backend interview questions.

Core subject in CS entrance exams. ~25 points.

docs.title: Relational Model and SQL
docs.text: • Relational model, relational algebra, SQL

docs.title: Storage and Indexing
docs.text: • B+ tree indexes, hash indexes, clustered indexes

docs.title: Query Processing and Optimization
docs.text: • Execution plans, join algorithms, query optimizer

docs.title: Transaction Processing
docs.text: • ACID, concurrency control, isolation levels, MVCC

docs.title: Crash Recovery
docs.text: • WAL, ARIES algorithm, checkpointing

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Balance theory and practice. Reading without writing SQL means concepts stay on paper.

Start with a specific database. Recommended: MySQL.

Do the CMU 15-445 labs. Implement a complete database system.

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description CMU 15-445 Course CMU database systems, implement a complete database Berkeley CS186 Course Berkeley database course DDIA Textbook Designing Data-Intensive Applications

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: UC Berkeley
  • Prerequisites: CS61B (Data Structures), CS61C (Architecture) or equivalent
  • Programming Language: Java
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Website: cs186berkeley.net

Berkeley CS186 is Berkeley's database systems course, covering relational models, SQL, storage and indexing, query processing, transaction processing, and concurrency control.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: cs186berkeley.net
  • Lectures: YouTube

docs.title: Course Features
docs.text:
• Java implementation: Easier to work with than C++
  • Project-driven: Build a simple database through multiple projects
  • Comprehensive coverage: From storage to query to transaction layers

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Learn Java basics first. The course uses Java; familiarity with collections and OOP is required.

Do all projects. Each project helps you understand a core database component.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: CS186 is an excellent course for getting started with databases. If you want to implement database components in Java, this is the best choice.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: Carnegie Mellon University (Andy Pavlo)
  • Prerequisites: C++ basics, data structures, operating systems basics
  • Programming Language: C++
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Website: 15445.courses.cs.cmu.edu

CMU 15-445 is CMU's database systems course, taught by Professor Andy Pavlo. It's widely regarded as one of the best database courses in the world — not because it covers the most material, but because it has you actually implement a complete database management system.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: 15445.courses.cs.cmu.edu
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Lab code: BusTub (the teaching database used in the course)

docs.title: Lab List
docs.text: Lab Content Difficulty Lab 1: Buffer Pool Manager Implement a buffer pool manager 🌟🌟🌟 Lab 2: B+ Tree Index Implement a B+ tree index 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 3: Query Execution Implement a query executor 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 4: Concurrency Control Implement concurrency control (lock manager + deadlock detection) 🌟🌟🌟🌟🌟

docs.title: Course Features
docs.text:
• Complete database implementation: Build a fully functional DBMS from scratch
  • BusTub: A purpose-built teaching database framework with manageable code size
  • 4 core labs: Cover the four core components of a database

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Solid C++ required. The course uses C++; familiarity with pointers, memory management, and templates is essential.

Do labs in order. Each lab depends on the previous lab's code.

Read the lecture notes. Andy Pavlo's lecture notes are excellent and key to understanding the theory.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
15-445 has the best lab design in database courses. After completing all labs, your understanding of databases will be transformed.

Andy Pavlo is a top database researcher, and his lecture videos are exceptionally high quality — highly recommended.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Martin Kleppmann (University of Cambridge)
  • Prerequisites: Database fundamentals, networking fundamentals, OS fundamentals
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

Designing Data-Intensive Applications (DDIA) by Martin Kleppmann is considered the "bible" of backend development by countless engineers.

The book's theme: how to design and build large-scale, reliable, scalable data systems. It's not a tutorial for any single technology — it builds comprehensive understanding of distributed data systems.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official: dataintensive.net
  • Lectures: Martin Kleppmann Lectures

docs.title: Part 1: Foundations of Data Systems
docs.text:
• Data models and query languages
  • Storage and retrieval (B+ trees, LSM trees, column stores)
  • Encoding and evolution (JSON, Protobuf, Avro, Thrift)

docs.title: Part 2: Distributed Data
docs.text:
• Replication (primary-replica, multi-leader, leaderless)
  • Partitioning (hash, range)
  • Distributed transactions and consensus (2PC, Paxos, Raft)

docs.title: Part 3: Derived Data
docs.text:
• Batch processing (MapReduce, Spark)
  • Stream processing (Kafka, Flink)
  • The future of data systems

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Build foundations first. The book assumes knowledge of basic databases and networking.

Read by topic, not sequentially. Choose chapters based on your needs.

Do the review questions. Each chapter has questions to test your understanding.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
DDIA is essential reading for backend engineers. Without understanding it, you can only call APIs; with it, you can design systems.

It's also a reference for system design interviews at many major companies.

docs.title: What are Data Structures and Algorithms
docs.text:
Data structures are ways of organizing and storing data. Arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, hash tables — each has its own characteristics suited for different scenarios.

Algorithms are step-by-step procedures for solving problems. Sorting, searching, traversal, dynamic programming, greedy algorithms — each is a general solution for a class of problems.

docs.title: Why Learn Data Structures and Algorithms
docs.text:
Programming fundamentals. DS&A are the foundation of all programming tasks. Without them, you might use a list where a hash table is needed, with orders of magnitude worse performance.

Interview core requirement. Algorithm problems are mandatory in tech company interviews. Solving hundreds of LeetCode problems is practically standard.

Develop computational learning. Learning algorithms isn't just about problem-solving techniques — it's about decomposing problems, abstract modeling, and analyzing complexity.

Understand AI and systems. Search engines use graph algorithms, databases use B+ trees, compilers use syntax trees, recommendation systems use graphs and matrices.

docs.title: Basic Data Structures
docs.text:
• Arrays vs. linked lists: contiguous storage vs. pointer linking
  • Stacks vs. queues: LIFO vs. FIFO
  • Hash tables: key-value mapping, collision handling, O(1) average lookup
  • Trees: binary trees, BST, AVL, red-black trees
  • Graphs: adjacency matrix vs. list, directed vs. undirected

docs.title: Basic Algorithms
docs.text:
• Sorting: bubble, selection, insertion, merge, quicksort, heap sort
  • Search: linear, binary, BFS, DFS
  • Recursion: recursive thinking, recursion to iteration

docs.title: Advanced Topics
docs.text:
• Dynamic programming: optimal substructure, overlapping subproblems, state transitions
  • Greedy algorithms: conditions for local-to-global optimality
  • Divide and conquer: break large problems into smaller ones
  • Graph algorithms: shortest path (Dijkstra, Floyd), MST (Prim, Kruskal), topological sort
  • Advanced data structures: heaps, union-find, segment trees, Trie

docs.title: Complexity Analysis
docs.text:
• Time complexity: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2)
  • Space complexity: how much extra memory an algorithm needs
  • Best/worst/average case: performance under different inputs

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Learn data structures first, then algorithms. Data structures are the carrier of algorithms. Without understanding linked lists, it's hard to understand linked list sorting.

Solve problems, but think while solving. After each problem: what data structure was used? What algorithm? What's the time complexity?

Solve by topic, not randomly. Finish array and string problems first, then linked lists, then trees, then dynamic programming.

Draw diagrams. Linked list pointer changes, tree rotations, graph traversals — diagrams are far more effective than text alone.

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description Berkeley CS61B Course Berkeley data structures with Java labs MIT 6.006 Course MIT introduction to algorithms Princeton Algorithms Course Princeton algorithms course, taught by Sedgewick VisuAlgo Tool Data structure and algorithm visualizations

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
CS61B is UC Berkeley's classic data structures course, taught by Professor Josh Hug. Using Java as the teaching language, the course has you implement a wide range of data structures and algorithms through carefully designed labs and projects. The emphasis is "learning by doing" — you'll build lists, trees, graphs, hash tables, and sorting algorithms from scratch.

CS61B has an outstanding reputation in the CS self-study community and is widely regarded as one of the best introductory data structures courses available. Its lab design is exceptional — you'll understand the tradeoffs between arrays and linked lists while implementing a Deque, and grasp hashing and graph traversal while building a Git version control system prototype.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Programming fundamentals: Familiarity with at least one programming language (Java preferred but not required). CS61A or equivalent programming experience
  • Basic OOP concepts: classes, objects, inheritance, interfaces
  • No prior algorithms or data structures knowledge required

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟⭐ (Medium)

The first half (basic data structures and Java syntax) is relatively accessible. The second half (graph algorithms, sorting, concurrency) ramps up significantly. Project 2B (building a Git prototype) and Project 3 (building a game engine) are the course highlights and require substantial time investment.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 120-150 hours (including labs and projects)

  • Lectures: ~40 hours
  • Labs: ~20 hours
  • Projects: ~50-80 hours
  • Review and exam prep: ~10-20 hours

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Course website: sp21.datastructur.es
  • Lecture videos: YouTube playlist
  • Code and labs: GitHub
  • Textbook: Hug's Data Structures (free online)

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Do the labs and projects. Watching lectures is just warm-up — real learning happens when you write code. CS61B's labs are exceptionally well-designed, each centered around a core concept.

  2. Don't skip Project 2 (Deque). This project asks you to implement a deque using both arrays and linked lists, making it the best exercise for understanding how data structure choice affects performance.

  3. Project 3 (Ngordnet) is optional but highly recommended. You'll build an N-gram-based historical trend query tool involving graph algorithms and data processing — genuinely fun.

  4. Use the provided JUnit tests. CS61B provides complete test frameworks for every lab and project. Run the tests first, see which ones fail, then debug targetedly.

  5. If you're not comfortable with Java, spend 1-2 weeks on Java basics first. The course assumes Java proficiency, and struggling with syntax early on is painful.

  6. Pair with Algorithms, 4th Edition by Sedgewick. The book and CS61B content are highly complementary, with many chapters directly corresponding.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
MIT 6.006 is MIT's introductory algorithms course, taught by Professors Erik Demaine, Srini Devadas, and Nancy Lynch. The course systematically covers fundamental methods of algorithm design and analysis, including sorting, searching, graph algorithms, dynamic programming, and greedy algorithms.

Unlike CS61B which emphasizes hands-on implementation, MIT 6.006 focuses more on understanding and analysis. The course emphasizes mathematical proofs and complexity analysis — you need to not only know how to use an algorithm, but also prove why it's correct and analyze its time complexity. This theoretical training is essential for deeply understanding the nature of algorithms.

The course uses Python as the teaching language, lowering the syntax barrier so you can focus on the algorithms themselves.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Programming fundamentals: Familiarity with Python or at least one programming language
  • Basic mathematics: High school math level, comfortable with logarithms, exponents, summations
  • Discrete math (recommended): Basic set theory, logic, mathematical induction

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟⭐ (Medium-Upper)

The theoretical portions (complexity proofs, correctness proofs for dynamic programming) can be challenging for students without a strong math background. However, the lectures are very clearly explained, and with videos and notes you can keep up.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 80-100 hours

  • Lectures: ~30 hours
  • Problem sets: ~30-40 hours
  • Review and exam prep: ~20-30 hours

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Course website: MIT OCW 6.006
  • Lecture videos: MIT OCW YouTube
  • Lecture notes and assignments: Complete lecture notes and problem sets available on the course website
  • Textbook: Introduction to Algorithms (CLRS) as reference

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Do the problem sets. MIT's problem sets are exceptionally well-designed. Watching videos without doing problems means you haven't really learned.

  2. Take complexity analysis seriously. This is the core skill of MIT 6.006. For every algorithm you learn, be able to state and prove its time and space complexity.

  3. Dynamic programming is the watershed. Many students get stuck here. Start by understanding "optimal substructure" and "overlapping subproblems," then practice with classic DP problems (knapsack, longest common subsequence, matrix chain multiplication).

  4. Pair with LeetCode practice. After finishing a topic, find corresponding problems on LeetCode. The theory + practice combination works best.

  5. If CLRS is too hard, read Algorithms, 4th Edition first. Sedgewick's book is more intuitive and works great as supplementary reading for MIT 6.006.

  6. Use the MIT OCW recitation videos. These problem-solving sessions are extremely helpful for understanding the problem sets.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Princeton Algorithms is a Coursera course by Princeton professors Robert Sedgewick and Kevin Wayne, split into two parts: Part I covers fundamental data structures and sorting, while Part II covers graph algorithms and string processing.

The course's greatest strength is its perfect alignment with the Algorithms, 4th Edition textbook. Sedgewick is both the course instructor and the textbook author, so the course content and book are highly consistent. All algorithms are implemented in Java, with extensive visualizations and interactive demos that make abstract algorithms tangible.

The course is available on Coursera with auto-graded programming assignments. The assignments are brilliantly designed — for example, implementing a sliding-window maximum using a deque, or building a range search with KD-Trees.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Java fundamentals: The course uses Java for all implementations; you need to be comfortable with Java syntax
  • Programming experience: At least one introductory programming course
  • No prior algorithms or data structures knowledge required

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟⭐ (Medium)

Part I is relatively accessible; Part II's graph algorithms and string processing are more challenging. The programming assignments are demanding but come with thorough hints and test cases.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 60-80 hours (Part I + Part II combined)

  • Part I: ~30-40 hours
  • Part II: ~30-40 hours

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Coursera Part I: Algorithms, Part I
  • Coursera Part II: Algorithms, Part II
  • Textbook: Algorithms, 4th Edition (Sedgewick & Wayne)
  • Companion website: algs4.cs.princeton.edu
  • Visualizations: Algorithms Visualizer

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Read the book before watching videos. Sedgewick's book is very clearly written. Read the corresponding chapter first, then watch the video for deeper understanding.

  2. Do the programming assignments. Coursera's auto-graded assignments provide instant feedback. The assignment design is often more educational than the exams.

  3. Use the visualization tools. Princeton provides extensive algorithm visualizations that are extremely helpful for understanding how sorting, tree operations, and graph traversals work step by step.

  4. Part II's string processing is worth special attention. KMP, Rabin-Karp, Trie, and suffix arrays are widely used in practice but skipped by many courses.

  5. If time is limited, prioritize Part I. Part I covers the data structures and algorithms most frequently tested in interviews — highest ROI.

  6. Pair with LeetCode practice. After each topic, solve 5-10 corresponding problems on LeetCode to solidify your understanding.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
VisuAlgo is a data structure and visualization tool developed by Professor Steven Halim from the National University of Singapore. It's not a traditional "course" but an interactive website that lets you see the dynamic execution process of various data structures and algorithms.

On VisuAlgo, you can watch: step-by-step element swaps in sorting algorithms, BST insertion and deletion processes, how Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths one step at a time, how BFS/DFS traverses a graph, how hash tables handle collisions — these animated demonstrations make abstract algorithms tangible and intuitive.

For self-learners, VisuAlgo is one of the best supplementary tools available. After learning an algorithm, watching the animation here helps you connect the code with the algorithm's execution process.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: None. VisuAlgo is suitable for learners at any stage.

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟 (Beginner)

This is a tool, not a course. The barrier to entry is extremely low — just open the website and use it.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text: Use as needed. Recommended: spend 10-15 minutes on VisuAlgo after learning each algorithm to watch the corresponding animation.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Website: visualgo.net
  • Languages: Chinese, English, and more
  • Topics covered: sorting, linked lists, stacks, queues, hash tables, BST, AVL trees, heaps, graphs, union-find, dynamic programming, and more

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Watch the animation right after learning an algorithm. For example, after learning quicksort, go to VisuAlgo and watch a complete sorting animation — you'll gain a whole new understanding of the partition process.

  2. Input your own data. VisuAlgo lets you enter custom data and watch how the algorithm processes it. This is more educational than watching preset demonstrations.

  3. Use alongside problem-solving. When you encounter an algorithm problem on LeetCode that you can't solve, go to VisuAlgo first to watch the corresponding algorithm demo, then come back to the problem.

  4. Focus on graph algorithm visualizations. Graph algorithms (Dijkstra, BFS/DFS, topological sort, minimum spanning trees) are the hardest to visualize in your head — VisuAlgo's animations are most valuable here.

  5. Don't just watch — implement. VisuAlgo is a supplement, not a replacement for writing code. After watching the animation, close the website and implement the algorithm yourself.

docs.title: What is Machine Learning
docs.text: Machine Learning is a core branch of AI that studies how computers can automatically learn patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed. Recommendation systems, spam filters, speech recognition — all powered by ML.

docs.title: Why Learn Machine Learning
docs.text:
Fundamental skill for AI engineers. Deep learning, reinforcement learning, and LLMs are all built on ML foundations.

Interview core. Bias-variance tradeoff, overfitting, gradient descent — these are high-frequency AI interview topics.

Foundation for understanding AI systems. Without ML understanding, you can only call APIs; with it, you can judge why models work or fail.

docs.title: Fundamental Concepts
docs.text:
• Supervised vs. unsupervised vs. semi-supervised vs. self-supervised learning
  • Training / validation / test sets
  • Overfitting and underfitting
  • Bias-Variance Tradeoff
  • Cross-Validation
  • Evaluation metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, AUC-ROC

docs.title: Classic Algorithms
docs.text:
• Linear regression, logistic regression
  • Decision trees, random forests, XGBoost
  • SVM, KNN, Naive Bayes
  • K-Means clustering, PCA

docs.title: Model Tuning
docs.text:
• Regularization: L1, L2, Dropout
  • Feature engineering
  • Hyperparameter tuning
  • Ensemble methods

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Start with Andrew Ng, then do Kaggle. Andrew Ng's course builds solid theory; Kaggle transforms it into practice.

Implement from scratch with NumPy. Don't just call scikit-learn APIs.

Learn by topic. Linear models first, then trees, then clustering.

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description Andrew Ng Machine Learning Course Andrew Ng ML course, best starting point Hung-Yi Lee Machine Learning Course Hung-Yi Lee ML course, Chinese lectures Watermelon Book Textbook Zhou Zhihua Machine Learning PRML Textbook Bishop Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Stanford / Coursera (Andrew Ng)
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, linear algebra, probability
  • Programming Language: Python (NumPy)
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

Andrew Ng's Machine Learning course is the most popular ML course in the world, period. Andrew Ng is a legend in AI — founder of Google Brain, co-founder of Coursera, former head of Baidu AI.

The course's greatest strength is its exceptional clarity. Andrew Ng explains complex math concepts intuitively, making them accessible to complete beginners.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: Coursera (free to audit)
  • New version: Machine Learning Specialization (3 courses, updated 2022)
  • Lecture notes: CS229 (Stanford original)

docs.title: Part 1: Supervised Learning
docs.text:
• Linear regression (single and multivariate)
  • Logistic regression
  • Regularization (L1, L2)
  • Neural network basics

docs.title: Part 2: Unsupervised Learning
docs.text:
• K-Means clustering
  • Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
  • Anomaly detection

docs.title: Part 3: Applications
docs.text:
• Recommendation systems
  • Large-scale machine learning

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Do all programming assignments. The course implements algorithms in Python/NumPy without libraries. This is the most valuable learning experience.

Don't skip the math derivations. Andrew Ng's math is exceptionally clear; following the derivations is more effective than watching videos ten times.

Practice on Kaggle. After learning each algorithm, find a corresponding dataset on Kaggle to practice.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This is the best choice for ML beginners. If you only take one ML course, take this one.

The new version (Machine Learning Specialization) uses Python, making it more practical than the old version (Octave/MATLAB).

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: National Taiwan University (Hung-Yi Lee)
  • Prerequisites: Python basics, linear algebra, probability
  • Programming Language: Python
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 50+

Hung-Yi Lee is a professor at NTU. His ML course is the highest-quality Chinese ML course available. It's updated yearly, covering both traditional ML and cutting-edge deep learning.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Lectures: Bilibili
  • Website: speech.ee.ntu.edu.tw
  • Assignments: Updated yearly, available on course website

docs.title: Course Features
docs.text:
• Chinese lectures: Fluent language with clear concept explanations
  • Cutting-edge content: Covers Transformers, GANs, BERT, GPT, and more
  • Updated yearly: Content continuously updated to follow AI frontier
  • Rich assignments: Each topic has accompanying programming assignments

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Pair with Andrew Ng. Andrew Ng covers foundations; Hung-Yi Lee covers cutting-edge topics. They complement each other.

Do the course assignments. Hung-Yi Lee's assignments are well-designed for deep understanding.

Watch the latest year's lectures. Content is updated annually; watch the most recent version.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Hung-Yi Lee's ML course is the best choice for Chinese-language ML learning. If you want to learn ML in Chinese, this is the top pick.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Christopher Bishop (Microsoft Research)
  • Prerequisites: Linear algebra, probability, calculus
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

Bishop's Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning (PRML) is a classic ML textbook that takes a probabilistic perspective throughout.

The book's hallmark: viewing all ML algorithms through the lens of probability theory. Linear regression, logistic regression, neural networks, SVMs — in Bishop's framework, they're all special cases of probabilistic models.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official site: Microsoft Research
  • Free PDF: Available on official website
  • Course: Cambridge ML Course

docs.title: Part 1: Probability Foundations
docs.text:
• Probability distributions
  • Linear regression (Bayesian perspective)
  • Linear classification

docs.title: Part 2: Core Models
docs.text:
• Neural networks
  • Kernel methods (SVM)
  • Sparse kernel machines

docs.title: Part 3: Advanced Topics
docs.text:
• Mixture models and EM
  • Approximate inference
  • Sampling methods
  • Continuous latent variables (PCA, factor analysis)
  • Combinatorial models

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Requires strong math foundations. PRML has high math requirements: probability, linear algebra, and calculus.

Pair with Andrew Ng. Build intuition with Andrew Ng first, then go deep with PRML.

Focus on the first 5 chapters. They cover core ML concepts; later chapters can be read selectively.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
PRML is one of the best textbooks for advanced ML theory. Essential if you want to deeply understand the probabilistic foundations of ML.

However, it's not for complete beginners. Start with Andrew Ng, then read PRML.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Zhou Zhihua (Nanjing University)
  • Prerequisites: Linear algebra, probability, calculus
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

Zhou Zhihua's Machine Learning (nicknamed the "Watermelon Book") is the most classic ML textbook in China. The name comes from the author's use of watermelon data as a running example throughout the book.

The book covers core ML algorithms and theory, comprehensive but theory-heavy. It's suitable for systematic ML theory study but not for complete beginners.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Book: Douban
  • Companion: Pumpkin Book (detailed formula derivations)
  • Lectures: Bilibili

docs.title: Part 1: Foundations
docs.text:
• Model evaluation and selection
  • Linear models
  • Decision trees

docs.title: Part 2: Core Algorithms
docs.text:
• Neural networks
  • Support Vector Machines
  • Bayesian classifiers
  • Ensemble learning

docs.title: Part 3: Advanced Topics
docs.text:
• Clustering
  • Dimensionality reduction
  • Feature selection and sparsity
  • Computational learning theory

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Use with the Pumpkin Book. The Watermelon Book's formula derivations are brief; the Pumpkin Book provides detailed derivations.

Read after Andrew Ng. The Watermelon Book is theory-heavy. Build intuition with Andrew Ng's course first.

Do the exercises. Each chapter has exercises to test your understanding.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
The Watermelon Book is a reference for ML graduate entrance exams and interviews in China. Essential for systematic ML theory study.

However, it's not suitable for complete beginners. Start with Andrew Ng's course, then read the Watermelon Book.

docs.title: What is Deep Learning
docs.text: Deep Learning is the engine of the current AI revolution. From GPT to Stable Diffusion, from AlphaFold to autonomous driving — all built on deep learning. It's a branch of machine learning using multi-layer neural networks to learn hierarchical representations.

docs.title: Why Learn Deep Learning
docs.text:
Core weapon of AI. Understanding deep learning is essential for understanding LLMs, multimodal AI, and generative AI.

Interview must-know. CNN, RNN, Transformer, attention mechanisms — high-frequency AI interview topics.

Foundation for research. Deep learning is essential for AI research.

docs.title: Neural Network Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Perceptron and MLP
  • Backpropagation
  • Activation functions: ReLU, Sigmoid, Tanh, GELU
  • Optimization: SGD, Momentum, Adam, AdamW
  • Regularization: Dropout, Batch Normalization

docs.title: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)
docs.text:
• Convolution, pooling
  • Classic architectures: LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, ResNet

docs.title: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN)
docs.text: • RNN, LSTM, GRU

docs.title: Transformer Architecture
docs.text:
• Self-Attention
  • Multi-Head Attention
  • BERT, GPT, ViT

docs.title: Generative Models
docs.text: • VAE, GAN, Diffusion Models

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Start with Andrew Ng, then read d2l. Andrew Ng builds the framework; d2l helps you implement in PyTorch.

Implement from scratch with PyTorch. Don't just call high-level APIs.

Do the CS231n labs. Implement CNNs from scratch.

Andrew Ng's Deep Learning Specialization is the most popular DL course. 5 courses from neural network basics to CNNs/RNNs.

  • Course 2: Hyperparameter Tuning, Regularization, Optimization
  • Course 3: Structured ML Projects
  • Course 4: CNNs
  • Course 5: Sequence Models (RNN, LSTM)

docs.title: Tips
docs.text: Do all programming assignments. The course uses Python/TensorFlow without high-level APIs.

CS231n is Stanford's computer vision course and one of the best DL courses. Covers CNN, RNN, GAN, Transformer, object detection, segmentation.

docs.text: CS231n is one of the best courses for DL beginners. Essential for understanding CNNs and computer vision.

Deep Learning (Flower Book) is the "bible" of deep learning, written by three top DL researchers. The "flower" comes from the flower on the cover.

Not for complete beginners. Start with Andrew Ng, then read the Flower Book.
