docs.title: What are Programming Languages
docs.text:
Programming languages are the bridge between humans and computers. Code you write in a programming language is ultimately translated into machine instructions that the CPU can execute. Different programming languages have different design philosophies, syntax, and use cases.

Learning a programming language isn't just about syntax. Each language embodies a philosophy about "how programs should be written." C lets you directly manipulate memory, emphasizing efficiency and control; Python manages memory for you, emphasizing simplicity and readability; Haskell uses pure functions and immutable data, emphasizing correctness and composability. Understanding these philosophies is far more important than memorizing syntax.

docs.title: Why Learn Programming Languages
docs.text:
Different tasks need different languages. There is no universal programming language. Operating systems are written in C/Rust, data science uses Python/R, frontend uses JavaScript/TypeScript, Android uses Kotlin, and iOS uses Swift. Mastering multiple languages lets you choose the best tool for each task.

Understand programming paradigms. Programming languages can be broadly categorized into paradigms: imperative (C, Java), functional (Haskell, Erlang), logic (Prolog), and object-oriented (Java, C++). Learning languages from different paradigms broadens your thinking and lets you view programming problems from different angles.

Learning new languages gets faster. The more languages you know, the easier it is to learn new ones. Most languages share fundamental concepts (variables, functions, loops, conditionals), with differences mainly in syntax and features. Once you understand multiple paradigms, learning a new language may take only days.

Deepen computer science understanding. Programming language design involves type systems, memory management, concurrency models, and compiler theory — core CS topics. Learning languages is also learning these underlying fundamentals.

docs.title: Programming Paradigms
docs.text:
• Imperative/Procedural: C, Go — change program state through a series of statements
  • Object-Oriented: Java, C++, Python — organize code using objects and classes
  • Functional: Haskell, Erlang, Scala — program with pure functions and immutable data
  • Multi-paradigm: Python, JavaScript, Rust — support multiple programming styles

docs.title: Type Systems
docs.text:
• Static vs dynamic typing: compile-time checking vs runtime checking
  • Strong vs weak typing: strictness of type conversions
  • Type inference: compiler automatically deduces types (e.g., Rust, Haskell)
  • Generics/parametric types: writing type-safe generic code

docs.title: Memory Management
docs.text:
• Manual management: C/C++ — you allocate and free memory yourself
  • Garbage Collection (GC): Java, Python, Go — runtime automatically reclaims unused memory
  • Ownership system: Rust — guarantees memory safety at compile time without GC

docs.title: Concurrency Models
docs.text:
• Threads and locks: Java, C++ — traditional concurrency model
  • Actor model: Erlang — concurrency through message passing
  • Coroutines: Go (goroutines), Python (asyncio) — lightweight concurrency
  • Async/await: JavaScript, Python, Rust — event-loop-based asynchronous programming

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Master one language first, then learn others. Don't try to learn many languages at once. Get one language to the point where you can solve real problems with it, then learn a second. Python is recommended as a first language — clean syntax, rich ecosystem, great for beginners.

Learn through projects. Don't just read syntax books. Set a small goal — build a web scraper, make a CLI tool, create a website — and learn the language's features and best practices through practice.

Read excellent source code. One of the best ways to learn a language is to read well-written code in that language. Python's standard library, Go's standard library, and Rust's crates are all great learning materials.

Focus on language design philosophy. Don't just learn "how to use it" — learn "why it was designed this way." Why does Rust have an ownership system? Why doesn't Go have inheritance? Why do functional languages emphasize immutability? Understanding design motivations is how you truly master a language.

Compare languages. Implement the same task (like a simple HTTP server) in different languages and compare the coding style, performance, and development experience. This comparison deepens your understanding of language features.

docs.title: Language Introductions
docs.text:
• Python Official Documentation — The go-to Python introduction
  • The Rust Programming Language — The official Rust tutorial, free to read online
  • A Tour of Go — Go's official interactive tutorial

docs.title: Programming Paradigms
docs.text:
• UW Programming Languages — UW course using SML, Racket, and Ruby to teach three paradigms
  • Concepts of Programming Languages (Sebesta) — Systematic introduction to various programming paradigms
  • Learn You a Haskell for Great Good — Classic introduction to functional programming

docs.title: Multi-Language Learning
docs.text:
• Exercism — Practice platform supporting 60+ languages with mentor code review
  • Rosetta Code — Side-by-side comparisons of the same task implemented in different languages

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
A Tour of Go is the official interactive tutorial for the Go programming language. You write and run Go code directly in your browser — no installation required. Written by the Go team, it's the fastest way to get started with Go.

Go (or Golang) is a statically-typed, compiled language developed by Google, known for its simplicity, efficiency, and concurrency support. Go's syntax is minimal (only 25 keywords), compilation is extremely fast, and built-in goroutines and channels make concurrent programming straightforward. It's ideal for building network services, microservices, CLI tools, and cloud-native applications.

Docker and Kubernetes are both written in Go. If you want to work in backend development or cloud-native infrastructure, Go is one of the essential languages to learn.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Basic programming concepts: Understanding of variables, functions, loops, etc.
  • No concurrency knowledge required (the tutorial teaches this from scratch)

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟 (Beginner to Intermediate)

Go's syntax has an extremely low barrier to entry. Concurrency is the more challenging part, but for most applications, understanding goroutines and channels is sufficient.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 10-15 hours (the tutorial itself) + project practice

  • Tour of Go tutorial: ~8-10 hours
  • Basic exercises: ~5-8 hours
  • Project practice: ongoing

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• English: go.dev/tour
  • Switch to Chinese via the website's language option
  • Go official docs: go.dev/doc
  • Go by Example: gobyexample.com — example-driven Go learning resource
  • Practice: Exercism Go Track

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Tour of Go is the fastest path to getting started. Writing code in the browser and seeing results instantly makes for extremely efficient learning.

  2. Master goroutines and channels. These are Go's core competitive advantages. Learn to use goroutines for concurrent execution and channels for safe communication between them.

  3. Go's interface design is unique. Go uses implicit interfaces (structural typing) — you don't explicitly declare which interface is implemented. This is more flexible than Java/C# interfaces but harder to grasp initially. Study multiple examples to internalize it.

  4. Don't get caught up in debates about Go's error handling style. Go uses if err != nil for error handling, which many people criticize. It's just Go's style — accept it.

  5. Recommended books:

  6. The Go Programming Language (Donovan & Kernighan) — the authoritative Go textbook

  7. Go in Action — a more practical introduction

  8. After finishing, build a small project: an HTTP API server, a concurrent web scraper, or a CLI tool. Go's standard library is very powerful — many features don't require third-party packages.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Python is an interpreted, object-oriented, high-level programming language released by Guido van Rossum in 1991. It is renowned for its concise and elegant syntax, using indentation instead of braces to define code blocks, making programs read as intuitive as English. Python's design philosophy is "elegance, clarity, simplicity," emphasizing code readability and development efficiency.

Python boasts an extremely rich standard library and a thriving third-party ecosystem, covering almost all fields such as web scraping, data analysis, artificial intelligence, web development, and automated operations. It is the de facto standard language in data science and AI, and one of the best choices for learning to program.

The Python official documentation is the most authoritative resource for learning Python, containing complete language references, standard library documentation, and tutorials.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• No prior experience needed. The Python official tutorial is designed for complete beginners
  • Basic computer skills (installing software, using the command line)

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟 (Beginner to Intermediate)

The beginner portion is very friendly; advanced topics (metaclasses, descriptors, concurrency models) are more challenging.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 40-60 hours (~10 hours for the tutorial; full mastery requires ongoing practice)

  • Official tutorial: ~10 hours
  • Standard library exploration: ~20-30 hours
  • Project practice: ongoing

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• English docs: docs.python.org/3.14/tutorial
  • Chinese docs: docs.python.org/zh-cn/3.14/tutorial
  • Standard library: docs.python.org/3/library
  • Python Cookbook: dabeaz-course.github.io/practical-python

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Start with the official tutorial and skip nothing. The Python official tutorial is the best introduction, covering all core language concepts.

  2. Master these key concepts: list comprehensions, generators, decorators, context managers, classes and inheritance. These are the essence of Python.

  3. Don't just read — write code. After learning every concept, try it immediately in the interpreter. Python's interactive interpreter is the best learning tool.

  4. Recommended books:

  5. Python Crash Course (Eric Matthes) — project-driven, great for beginners
  6. Automate the Boring Stuff with Python (Al Sweigart) — practical focus, free online

  7. Fluent Python (Luciano Ramalho) — deep dive into Python, for intermediate learners

  8. After learning the basics, pick one direction: Web dev (Django/Flask), data science (NumPy/Pandas), automation, AI/ML (PyTorch). Don't try to learn everything at once.

  9. Contribute to open source. Find a project on GitHub that interests you, read the code, and submit PRs — the best way to level up your programming skills.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
The Rust Programming Language (commonly known as "The Rust Book") is the official tutorial for the Rust programming language, written by the Rust community and available free online. Rust is a systems programming language developed by Mozilla, focused on memory safety and concurrency safety. It guarantees safety at compile time through its unique ownership system, without needing a garbage collector.

Rust has ranked #1 in Stack Overflow's "Most Loved Programming Language" survey for multiple consecutive years. It is widely used in operating systems, web servers, command-line tools, blockchain, game engines, and more. The Linux kernel has also started accepting Rust code.

This book is the best starting point for learning Rust, covering all core concepts from scratch: ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, type system, generics, traits, concurrency, and more.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Programming experience: Familiar with at least one programming language (C/C++ background is helpful but not required)
  • Basic command-line skills: Can compile and run programs from a terminal
  • No prior systems programming or memory management knowledge required

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟🌟🌟 (Challenging)

Rust's ownership system is its core innovation and also the biggest learning hurdle. Many programmers get stuck here for weeks. But once you understand ownership, the rest of Rust becomes much more natural.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 80-120 hours

  • Reading the book: ~40-60 hours
  • Exercises and projects: ~40-60 hours

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• English original: doc.rust-lang.org/book
  • Chinese translation: kaisery.github.io/trpl-zh-cn
  • Rust Playground (online compiler): play.rust-lang.org
  • Rustlings (interactive exercises): github.com/rust-lang/rustlings

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Read in order, don't skip chapters. Each chapter builds on the previous one, especially the ownership-related chapters (4-10).

  2. Don't get frustrated by compiler errors. Rust's compiler error messages are among the friendliest of any compiler — they typically tell you exactly what's wrong and how to fix it. Read them carefully; they are the best teachers for learning Rust.

  3. Use Rustlings for practice. Rustlings is a set of interactive small exercises, each targeting a core concept — perfect for reinforcing what you learned in the book.

  4. Don't try to understand lifetimes all at once. Lifetimes are Rust's hardest concept. Learn the basic usage first, then deepen your understanding through practice.

  5. After reading the book, build some small projects: a CLI tool, a simple HTTP parser, a file processing utility. Projects are the best way to solidify knowledge.

  6. Join the Rust community. The Rust community is known for being exceptionally friendly. Asking questions on Reddit, Discord, or the Rust forum usually yields patient, detailed answers.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Programming Languages is a Coursera course from the University of Washington, taught by Professor Dan Grossman. The course uses three languages — SML (Standard ML), Racket, and Ruby — to teach three different programming paradigms: functional, functional (with macros), and object-oriented.

The course's unique value is that it doesn't just teach you three languages — it uses them to help you understand core programming language concepts: pattern matching, recursion, closures, type inference, macros, polymorphism, subtyping vs parametric polymorphism. After this course, you'll be able to quickly identify the design patterns and core features of any new language you encounter.

Dan Grossman's teaching is rated by students as "unreasonably clear" — he makes complex concepts simple and accessible.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Programming experience: Familiar with at least one programming language
  • No prior knowledge of SML, Racket, or Ruby required (the course teaches them from scratch)
  • No functional programming experience required

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟⭐ (Medium)

The first half (SML basics) is relatively accessible. The second half (Racket's macro system, Ruby's object model) is more challenging. The programming assignments are demanding.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 50-70 hours

  • Video lectures: ~20 hours
  • Programming assignments: ~30-50 hours

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Coursera: Programming Languages
  • Course website: cs.washington.edu/education/courses/cse341
  • Textbook: Dan Grossman's course notes (free, available on the course website)

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Do the programming assignments. The assignments are the core of this course — skipping them means you haven't really learned. You'll implement various language features in SML, Racket, and Ruby, which is the best way to understand programming paradigms.

  2. Don't skip the SML section. Many students skip SML because they think it's not practical, but SML's pattern matching and type inference are the best entry point to understanding functional programming.

  3. Racket's macro system is worth special focus. Macros are Racket's most powerful feature and the key to understanding the Lisp philosophy of "code as data."

  4. After finishing, compare with languages you already know. For example, Java's generics are parametric polymorphism, while Python's duck typing is subtyping polymorphism. These comparisons deepen your understanding of language design.

  5. This course is best taken after learning your first language and before learning your second. It builds "meta-knowledge" about programming languages, making every subsequent language easier to learn.

  6. If time is limited, prioritize Part A (SML) and Part B (Racket). Part C (Ruby) is relatively independent and can be done later.

docs.title: What is Compiler Theory
docs.text: Compiler theory studies how to translate high-level programming languages into machine-executable instructions. A compiler is the program that performs this translation. It typically divides into phases: lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, intermediate code generation, optimization, and code generation.

docs.title: Why Learn Compiler Theory
docs.text:
Understand the nature of programming languages. You don't just "use" a language — you understand why it's designed the way it is.

Foundation for AI code generation. The AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) is the most fundamental data structure for AI-assisted programming.

Write better code. Understanding compiler optimizations helps you write compiler-friendly code.

Train hardcore engineering thinking. Compilers are among the most complex systems in software engineering.

docs.title: Lexical Analysis
docs.text:
• Regular expressions, finite automata (NFA/DFA)
  • Lexer: converts source code character stream into token sequences

docs.title: Parsing
docs.text:
• Context-Free Grammars (CFG)
  • Top-down parsing: recursive descent, LL(1)
  • Bottom-up parsing: LR(0), SLR, LALR, LR(1)
  • Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)

docs.title: Semantic Analysis
docs.text:
• Type systems: static vs. dynamic typing, type checking, type inference
  • Symbol tables: managing scope and attributes of identifiers

docs.title: Intermediate Code and Optimization
docs.text:
• Intermediate Representation (IR): three-address code, SSA
  • Optimization: constant folding, dead code elimination, loop optimization

docs.title: Code Generation
docs.text: • Instruction selection, register allocation, instruction scheduling

docs.title: Runtime Environment
docs.text: • Stack frame management, garbage collection, closure implementation

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Start with hands-on work, then dive into theory. Build a simple compiler first to build intuition.

Start with Crafting Interpreters. Build two programming languages from scratch, free online.

Do the Stanford CS143 labs. Implement a complete compiler (lexing, parsing, semantic analysis, code generation).

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description Stanford CS143 Course Stanford compiler course with complete labs Crafting Interpreters Tutorial Build two languages from scratch, free online Engineering a Compiler Textbook Classic compiler design textbook LLVM Tool Modern compiler infrastructure

docs.title: Crafting Interpreters: Build Two Languages from Scratch
Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Robert Nystrom
  • Prerequisites: Proficiency in at least one programming language
  • Programming Language: Java (Part 1), C (Part 2)
  • Difficulty: 🌟�🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

Crafting Interpreters is a free online tutorial by Robert Nystrom that teaches you to implement two programming languages (Lox) from scratch. Part 1 builds a tree-walking interpreter in Java; Part 2 builds a bytecode compiler and VM in C.

The book's greatest strength is its extreme practicality. Every chapter has complete code, and you can follow along to build a working interpreter and compiler step by step.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official site: craftinginterpreters.com
  • Online reading: Completely free
  • GitHub: craftinginterpreters (complete code)

docs.title: Part 1: Tree-Walking Interpreter (Java)
docs.text:
• Scanning (lexical analysis)
  • Parsing
  • Expression evaluation
  • Statements and control flow
  • Functions and closures
  • Classes and inheritance

docs.title: Part 2: Bytecode Compiler (C)
docs.text:
• Bytecode design
  • Compiler implementation
  • Virtual machine
  • Garbage collection
  • Optimization techniques

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Write code along with the tutorial, don't just read. The book's value is in hands-on implementation. After each chapter, you'll have a more capable interpreter/compiler.

Read Part 1 before Part 2. Part 1 uses Java and is easier to understand. Part 2 uses C and involves memory management and performance optimization.

Reference, don't copy. Implement first, then check the author's code when stuck.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Crafting Interpreters is the best hands-on tutorial for getting started with compilers. It doesn't replace a formal compiler course (like Stanford CS143), but it gives you the most intuitive understanding — building a programming language isn't that hard.

Many consider it one of the best programming books they've ever read.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Keith Cooper, Linda Torczon (Rice University)
  • Prerequisites: Data structures, computer architecture basics
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

Engineering a Compiler by Keith Cooper and Linda Torczon of Rice University is a compiler textbook on par with the "Dragon Book."

Unlike the Dragon Book's theory-heavy approach, this book focuses on engineering practice: how to implement various optimizations in real compilers, and how to balance compilation time versus generated code quality.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Publisher: Elsevier
  • Course: Rice University COMP 412

docs.title: Part 1: Frontend
docs.text:
• Lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis
  • Symbol table management
  • Intermediate Representation (IR)

docs.title: Part 2: Optimization
docs.text:
• Local: constant folding, algebraic simplification, dead code elimination
  • Global: data flow analysis, loop optimization
  • Interprocedural: inlining, escape analysis

docs.title: Part 3: Backend
docs.text:
• Instruction selection, register allocation, instruction scheduling
  • Code generation

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Read the Dragon Book or complete CS143 first. This book is for those with some compiler background.

Focus on the optimization section. Optimization is the most complex and valuable part of a compiler, and the heart of this book.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: This is one of the best textbooks for advanced compiler study. If you want to deeply understand modern compilers like GCC and LLVM, this book is essential reading.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Chris Lattner (creator of Swift, formerly Apple/Tesla/Modular)
  • Prerequisites: C++ basics, compiler theory basics
  • Programming Language: C++
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

LLVM is the de facto standard for modern compiler infrastructure. Clang, Swift, Rust, and Julia compilers are all built on LLVM.

Learning LLVM means: you're no longer just a compiler theory student — you can actually participate in modern compiler development. LLVM's modular design lets you add new optimization passes for existing languages or generate code for custom hardware.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official site: llvm.org
  • Official tutorial: LLVM Tutorial
  • Documentation: LLVM Documentation
  • GitHub: llvm/llvm-project

docs.title: LLVM IR
docs.text:
• LLVM's Intermediate Representation (IR) is its core
  • Three-address code in SSA (Static Single Assignment) form
  • Various optimizations can be performed at the IR level

docs.title: Pass Architecture
docs.text:
• LLVM optimizations are implemented as Passes
  • Each Pass does one thing (e.g., constant folding, loop unrolling)
  • Passes can be composed and reused

docs.title: Backend
docs.text:
• LLVM supports virtually all major CPU architectures (x86, ARM, RISC-V, GPU)
  • TableGen is used to describe target architectures

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Learn compiler theory first. Without understanding IR, optimization, and code generation, LLVM will be overwhelming.

Follow the official tutorial. LLVM provides a complete tutorial implementing a simple language (Kaleidoscope) and compiling to LLVM IR.

Read the Clang source code. Clang is LLVM's C/C++ frontend with excellent code quality — the best reference for learning compiler implementation.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
LLVM is the infrastructure for industrial-grade compiler development. If you want to work on compilers, programming languages, or AI compilers (like TVM, MLIR), LLVM is essential.

Chris Lattner founded Modular AI in 2022, building AI compiler infrastructure using the LLVM/MLIR technology stack.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: Stanford University
  • Prerequisites: CS106B (Data Structures), CS107 (Computer Organization) or equivalent
  • Programming Language: C++
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Website: web.stanford.edu/class/cs143/

Stanford CS143 is Stanford's compiler course, one of the best in the world. You implement a complete compiler in C++ targeting COOL (Classroom Object-Oriented Language), a teaching language.

The course covers the complete compiler pipeline: lexical analysis, parsing, semantic analysis, intermediate code generation, optimization, and code generation.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: web.stanford.edu/class/cs143/
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Assignments: 4 compiler projects (lexer, parser, semantic analyzer, code generator)

docs.title: Course Features
docs.text:
• Complete compiler implementation: Build a complete compiler from scratch
  • COOL language: A teaching language designed to be simple yet cover all core concepts
  • 4 programming projects: Each implements one compiler phase, combined into a complete compiler

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Learn C++ basics first. The course uses C++; familiarity with OOP is required.

Complete all 4 projects in order. Each depends on the previous; you can't skip ahead.

Read the provided course materials. The course website provides detailed readings essential for understanding the theory.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: CS143 is one of the best courses for getting started with compilers. After completing it, you'll understand how compilers for any programming language work.

docs.title: What is Computer Architecture
docs.text: Computer Architecture studies the organization of computer systems — how CPUs execute instructions, how memory stores and retrieves data, and how various components work together. The core question: how does a computer run the programs you write?

docs.title: Why Learn Computer Architecture
docs.text:
Understand why programs are fast or slow. Arrays are much faster than linked lists for the same algorithm — the reason is cache locality. With architecture knowledge, you can make informed optimization decisions.

Understand "abstraction leaks." Integer overflow, floating-point precision, concurrency races — the root causes are at the hardware level.

Foundation for embedded and systems programming. Essential for embedded development, device drivers, compilers, and performance optimization.

Key topic for exams and interviews. Instruction pipelines, cache principles, and bus arbitration are core exam and interview topics.

docs.title: Data Representation
docs.text:
• Binary and hexadecimal
  • Integer representation: sign-magnitude, one's complement, two's complement
  • Floating-point: IEEE 754
  • Character encoding: ASCII, UTF-8

docs.title: Instruction Set Architecture
docs.text:
• ISA: CISC vs RISC
  • Instruction types: arithmetic, logical, control flow, memory access
  • Assembly language: x86 or RISC-V

docs.title: CPU Design
docs.text:
• Datapath: ALU, registers, buses
  • Control unit: instructions to control signals
  • Pipelining: fetch, decode, execute, memory access, write-back
  • Pipeline hazards: data, control, structural

docs.title: Memory Hierarchy
docs.text:
• Cache: L1/L2/L3, mapping policies, replacement strategies
  • Main memory (DRAM): organization, bandwidth, latency
  • Virtual memory: address translation, TLB, page replacement
  • Memory locality: temporal and spatial

docs.title: Buses and I/O
docs.text:
• Bus types: data, address, control
  • I/O methods: programmed I/O, interrupts, DMA

docs.title: Parallel Processing
docs.text:
• ILP: superscalar, out-of-order execution
  • Data-Level Parallelism: SIMD, GPU
  • Thread-Level Parallelism: multi-core, shared memory

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Start with C and assembly. Write simple C programs and use gcc -S to see generated assembly.

Use simulation tools. Use Ripes or Venus to simulate RISC-V processor execution.

Draw datapath diagrams. Draw data flow between components during instruction execution.

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description Berkeley CS61C Course Berkeley architecture course with RISC-V labs Nand2Tetris Course From NAND gates to a complete computer CS:APP Textbook Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach Textbook Classic architecture textbook

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: CMU (Randal Bryant, David O'Hallaron)
  • Prerequisites: C programming basics
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

CS:APP (Computer Systems: A Programmer's Perspective) is CMU's classic textbook and one of the most popular computer systems books in the world. Its unique perspective: understanding computer systems from a programmer's point of view.

Unlike traditional architecture textbooks written for hardware designers, CS:APP focuses on: as a C programmer, what low-level knowledge do you need to write correct and efficient code?

The book covers the complete chain from data representation, assembly language, processor architecture, cache optimization, linking, virtual memory, system I/O, networking, to concurrency.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official site: csapp.cs.cmu.edu
  • Course: CMU 15-213 (complete videos and labs on course website)
  • Lab code: CS:APP Labs
  • Chinese translation: 《深入理解计算机系统》(3rd Edition)

docs.title: Part 1: Program Structure and Execution
docs.text: • Data representation, assembly, processor architecture, performance optimization

docs.title: Part 2: Running Programs on a System
docs.text: • Linking, exceptional control flow, virtual memory

docs.title: Part 3: Interaction and Communication Between Programs
docs.text: • System-level I/O, network programming, concurrency

docs.title: Lab List
docs.text: Lab Content Difficulty Lab 1: Data Lab Bit manipulation, integer/float representation 🌟🌟 Lab 2: Bomb Lab Assembly reverse engineering 🌟🌟🌟 Lab 3: Attack Lab Buffer overflow attacks 🌟🌟🌟 Lab 4: Architecture Lab Y86-64 processor design 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 5: Cache Lab Cache optimization 🌟🌟🌟 Lab 6: Shell Lab Implement a Unix shell 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 7: Malloc Lab Implement malloc/free 🌟🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 8: Proxy Lab Implement a web proxy 🌟🌟🌟🌟

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Do all the labs. CS:APP's labs are the soul of the book. Bomb Lab and Attack Lab are particularly classic, giving you real understanding of assembly and memory safety.

Watch the CMU 15-213 lectures. The lecture quality is exceptional; Professor Bryant explains concepts thoroughly.

Don't skip the assembly chapter. Chapter 3 (Machine-Level Representation) is the hardest but most important. Understanding x86-64 assembly is key to understanding the machine.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
CS:APP bridges "programming" and "systems." After reading it, you'll understand the complete journey from C code to machine execution.

This book is also a reference for many tech company interviews. Bomb Lab and Attack Lab are among the most frequently asked-about project experiences in interviews.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: UC Berkeley
  • Prerequisites: CS61A (Python), CS61B (Data Structures) or equivalent
  • Programming Language: C, RISC-V Assembly
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

Berkeley CS61C is Berkeley's computer architecture course, widely regarded as one of the best architecture courses in the world. It starts from high-level languages (C) and gradually goes down to the machine level (RISC-V assembly, digital logic, CPU design).

CS61C's greatest strength is its exceptional lab design. The course has 6 labs, progressing from C pointer operations to RISC-V CPU design. By the end, you'll have designed a CPU capable of running RISC-V instructions using Logisim.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: cs61c.org
  • Lectures: YouTube
  • Notes: Available on the course website
  • Textbook: Based on Computer Organization and Design: The Hardware/Software Interface (RISC-V Edition)

docs.title: Lab List
docs.text: Lab Content Difficulty Lab 1: C and pointers C basics, pointers, memory operations 🌟🌟 Lab 2: RISC-V assembly Assembly language, function calls 🌟🌟 Lab 3: Floating point IEEE 754, floating-point arithmetic 🌟🌟 Lab 4: CPU design Design RISC-V CPU in Logisim 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 5: SIMD optimization Vectorized computation 🌟🌟🌟 Lab 6: Cache optimization Cache-friendly code 🌟🌟🌟

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Master C and pointers first. CS61C has high C language requirements, especially pointers and memory management. Learn C well before starting.

Do the CPU design lab seriously. Lab 4 is the course climax — designing a RISC-V CPU from scratch in Logisim. This lab gives you a true understanding of how CPUs work.

Draw datapath diagrams. The CPU datapath is the course's core. Draw how each instruction executes through the datapath.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
CS61C is one of the best courses for architecture beginners. The lecture notes and lab guides on the course website are excellent, making self-study possible even without enrolling.

The course uses RISC-V (not x86), which is cleaner and easier to understand.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: John Hennessy, David Patterson (2017 Turing Award winners)
  • Prerequisites: Computer organization fundamentals
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 100+

Computer Architecture: A Quantitative Approach is the "bible" of computer architecture, authored by 2017 Turing Award winners John Hennessy and David Patterson.

This is not an introductory textbook but an authoritative reference for deep understanding of processor design and performance optimization. It uses a "quantitative" approach — evaluating different design choices through data and experiments — to explain every architectural decision.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official site: Hennessy & Patterson Books
  • Courses: CMU 15-740, Stanford CS149
  • Companion book: Computer Organization and Design: The Hardware/Software Interface (introductory version, RISC-V edition recommended)

docs.title: Part 1: Fundamentals
docs.text:
• Quantitative design principles
  • Memory hierarchy design
  • Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)

docs.title: Part 2: Data-Level Parallelism
docs.text:
• SIMD and vector processors
  • GPU architecture
  • Data-level parallel programming

docs.title: Part 3: Thread-Level Parallelism
docs.text:
• Multi-core processor architecture
  • Cache coherence protocols (MESI, MOESI)
  • Memory consistency models

docs.title: Part 4: Large-Scale Parallelism
docs.text:
• Warehouse-scale computers
  • Data center architecture
  • Interconnection networks

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Read Computer Organization and Design first. That's the introductory companion book. Build foundations before tackling this one.

Focus on quantitative analysis. The book's core isn't memorizing architectures — it's learning to evaluate design decisions with data and experiments.

Read accompanying papers. The book references many classic papers; read a few that interest you.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This book is for those who want to deeply study architecture. If you just need to pass exams or interviews, Computer Organization and Design is sufficient.

Hennessy and Patterson received the 2017 Turing Award for their contributions to RISC architecture. This book is the culmination of their life's work.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: Hebrew University (Noam Nisan, Shimon Schocken)
  • Prerequisites: None, beginner-friendly
  • Programming Language: HDL, Jack (Java-like), Assembly
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 80+

Nand2Tetris is a legendary course: starting from a NAND gate, build a complete computer system step by step, and ultimately run Tetris on it.

The philosophy: there's no "magic" in computer systems. From the lowest-level logic gates to the highest-level operating system and compiler, each layer can be built from the abstraction of the previous one. When you do this hands-on, you gain an inside-out understanding of computer systems.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Website: nand2tetris.org
  • Lectures: Coursera Part 1 | Coursera Part 2
  • Textbook: The Elements of Computing Systems (free PDF available)
  • Software: Download page

docs.title: Part 1: Hardware to Computer (Chapters 1-5)
docs.text:
1. Boolean logic: Build basic gates from NAND (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, MUX, DMUX)
  2. Boolean arithmetic: Build the ALU
  3. Sequential logic: Build registers and RAM
  4. Machine language: Design the instruction set
  5. Computer architecture: Combine all components into a complete Hack computer

docs.title: Part 2: Assembly to Compiler (Chapters 6-11)
docs.text:
1. Assembler: Translate assembly to machine code
  2. VM I: Stack-based VM implementation
  3. VM II: Program control flow
  4. High-level language: Jack language (Java-like)
  5. Compiler I: Syntax analysis
  6. Compiler II: Code generation
  7. Operating system: Standard libraries

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Don't skip any chapter. Each builds on the previous. Skip gates and you can't understand the ALU; skip the ALU and you can't understand the CPU.

Do the projects. The course centers on 12 projects — from designing a NAND gate to implementing Tetris. Each requires hand-on work; no shortcuts.

Pair with CS:APP. Nand2Tetris gives a bottom-up perspective; CS:APP gives a top-down perspective. Together, they provide a complete picture.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Nand2Tetris is the best introductory course for understanding computer systems. It doesn't replace specialized courses (architecture, OS, compilers), but it gives you a full-stack perspective of how every layer is built.

Many consider it the best CS course they've ever taken. After completing it, you'll have a completely new understanding of computers.

docs.title: What Is Computer Graphics
docs.text:
Computer graphics studies how to generate, process, and display visual content using computers. From UI rendering on your phone screen, to virtual worlds in 3D games, to visual effects in movies, to AI image generation — all of these rely on computer graphics knowledge.

The core problem of graphics: how to convert a 3D scene (models, materials, lighting, camera) into a 2D image (pixels). This process is called rendering. Rendering falls into two categories: real-time rendering (games, interactive applications, requiring 30-120 frames per second) and offline rendering (film VFX, pursuing extreme realism, where a single frame can take hours to render).

docs.title: Why Study Computer Graphics
docs.text:
Core of game development. You're building games with Godot 4. Understanding graphics helps you use the rendering pipeline, shaders, and lighting systems more effectively to create better visuals.

Foundation for AI image generation. The underlying principles of generative AI models (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E, Sora) — diffusion models, NeRF, 3D Gaussian Splatting — are all built on graphics.

Intersection of multimodal AI. Computer vision (helping AI understand images) and computer graphics (helping AI generate images) are two sides of the same coin.

Train mathematical application skills. Graphics is the most intuitive application of linear algebra, geometry, and calculus. Studying graphics helps you truly understand the practical meaning of matrix transformations, vector spaces, and probability distributions.

Frontier research directions. Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF), 3D Gaussian Splatting, real-time path tracing — these are the hottest research areas at the intersection of graphics and AI.

docs.title: Mathematical Foundations
docs.text:
• Linear algebra: Matrix transformations (translation, rotation, scaling), homogeneous coordinates, quaternions
  • Vector operations: Dot product (lighting calculations), cross product (normals), vector projection
  • Geometry: Parametric equations, Bezier curves, B-splines

docs.title: The Rasterization Pipeline
docs.text:
• Vertex processing: Model transform → View transform → Projection transform → Viewport transform
  • Triangle rasterization: Converting 3D triangles to 2D pixels
  • Depth buffer (Z-Buffer): Resolving occlusion relationships
  • Texture mapping: Applying 2D images to 3D model surfaces
  • Anti-aliasing: MSAA, FXAA

docs.title: Lighting and Shading
docs.text:
• Phong lighting model: Ambient + diffuse + specular
  • Blinn-Phong model: An improvement on Phong
  • PBR (Physically Based Rendering): Microfacet model, BRDF, energy conservation
  • Shadows: Shadow Mapping, Ray Traced Shadows
  • Global illumination: Ray tracing, radiosity, photon mapping

docs.title: Ray Tracing
docs.text:
• Ray generation: Cast rays from the camera through each pixel
  • Ray-object intersection: Spheres, triangles, AABB
  • Recursive ray tracing: Reflection, refraction, soft shadows
  • Acceleration structures: BVH (Bounding Volume Hierarchy), KD-Tree
  • Path tracing: Monte Carlo methods for solving the rendering equation

docs.title: Shader Programming
docs.text:
• Vertex shader: Processes position and attributes of each vertex
  • Fragment shader: Computes the final color of each pixel
  • Compute shader: General-purpose GPU computation
  • GLSL / HLSL: Shader programming languages

docs.title: Advanced Topics
docs.text:
• Geometry processing: Mesh simplification, subdivision surfaces, parameterization
  • Animation: Skeletal animation, skinning, keyframe interpolation, physics simulation
  • Volume rendering: Clouds, smoke, fire
  • Neural rendering: NeRF, 3D Gaussian Splatting

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Start with OpenGL/WebGL. Write a simple triangle rendering program and understand each stage of the graphics pipeline. Recommended: LearnOpenGL — excellent tutorial quality, available in Chinese.

Practice with Godot. You're already using Godot 4. Try writing custom shaders and understanding Godot's rendering pipeline. Combining graphics theory with game engine practice produces the best learning results.

Do Ray Tracing in One Weekend. This tutorial series implements a ray tracer in a few hundred lines of C++. You can finish it in three days, and the sense of accomplishment is incredible. It's the best hands-on project for getting started with graphics.

Don't skip the math. The math in graphics (linear algebra, geometry) is the foundation for understanding everything. Recommended supplement: 3Blue1Brown's Essence of Linear Algebra series.

docs.title: Systematic Courses
docs.text:
• Berkeley CS184 — Berkeley's graphics course with complete project assignments covering modeling, animation, and rendering
  • Stanford CS148 — Stanford's introductory graphics course
  • MIT 6.837 — MIT's computer graphics course

docs.title: Hands-on Tutorials (Highly Recommended)
docs.text:
• LearnOpenGL — The best OpenGL tutorial, available in Chinese, build a rendering pipeline from scratch
  • Ray Tracing in One Weekend — Build a ray tracer in three days, free online, best starting project
  • The Book of Shaders — Interactive shader programming tutorial
  • WebGL Fundamentals — WebGL tutorial that runs directly in the browser

docs.title: Textbooks
docs.text:
• PBRT — The authoritative text on offline rendering and path tracing, free online
  • Computer Graphics: Principles and Practice (Foley, van Dam et al.) — Classic graphics textbook, comprehensive coverage
  • Real-Time Rendering (Tomas Akenine-Möller et al.) — The bible of real-time rendering, required reading

docs.title: Frontier Directions
docs.text:
• NeRF: Neural Radiance Fields — The original NeRF paper
  • 3D Gaussian Splatting — 3D Gaussian Splatting, a breakthrough in real-time neural rendering
  • Two Minute Papers (YouTube) — Accessible explanations of cutting-edge graphics and AI research

docs.title: Berkeley CS184: Introduction to Computer Graphics
Introduction
docs.text:
UC Berkeley's computer graphics course, taught by Professor Ren Ng. The course covers the three core areas of graphics: Modeling, Animation, and Rendering. Through a series of programming projects, students build a complete graphics system from scratch, including 3D model loading, physics simulation, and ray tracing.

CS184 emphasizes both theory and practice: every concept corresponds to a hands-on project, helping you understand each stage of the graphics pipeline through implementation. The course uses C++ and OpenGL, requiring students to have some programming foundation.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• C++ programming skills: Course projects use C++; familiarity with pointers, memory management, and OOP is required
  • Linear algebra: Matrix transformations and vector operations are fundamental mathematical tools in graphics
  • Calculus: Understanding lighting models and physics simulation requires basic calculus
  • Data structures: Trees and graphs are widely used in scene graphs and acceleration structures

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟🌟🌟 (Intermediate-High)

The course has a certain math and programming barrier, but explanations are clear and projects are well-designed. Best suited for learners with some prior foundation.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 80-100 hours (15-week course, ~5-7 hours per week)

  • Lectures: 2 hours/week
  • Programming projects: 3-5 hours/week (5 projects total)
  • Reading and review: 1-2 hours/week

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Course homepage: inst.eecs.berkeley.edu/~cs184/
  • Lecture videos: Some semesters available on YouTube
  • Project code: Open-source project frameworks on GitHub

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Complete every project in order. CS184's projects are progressive — each builds on the last. Don't skip any.

  2. Understand the math before writing code. Before starting each project, make sure you understand the underlying mathematical principles. For example, before doing ray tracing, fully grasp the geometric derivation of ray-triangle intersection.

  3. Use debugging tools wisely. Graphics debugging is painful — a misaligned matrix can make the entire scene disappear. Learn to use printf debugging, verifying the result of each transformation step by step.

  4. Reference Real-Time Rendering. The course's recommended textbook Real-Time Rendering is the bible of real-time rendering. Read relevant chapters alongside the course.

  5. Combine with Godot practice. Since you're building games with Godot 4, try applying rendering techniques from CS184 to Godot's custom shaders to deepen your understanding.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
LearnOpenGL is widely recognized as the best OpenGL tutorial for beginners, written by Joey de Vries. The tutorial starts from the most basic triangle rendering and gradually advances to advanced topics like PBR, deferred rendering, and shadow mapping.

The tutorial's greatest strength is that every concept comes with corresponding code examples — you can learn by doing, building a complete rendering engine from scratch. The tutorial uses C++ and OpenGL, and a Chinese version is available, making it very accessible for Chinese readers.

LearnOpenGL covers the core content of modern OpenGL (3.3+), including: OpenGL basics, lighting models, model loading, anti-aliasing, and advanced OpenGL techniques. After completing this tutorial, you'll be capable of real-time rendering with OpenGL.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• C++ basics: Familiarity with C++ syntax including classes, pointers, and STL containers
  • Basic linear algebra: Fundamental concepts of matrices and vectors (no deep knowledge required; the tutorial includes a review)
  • Basic computer graphics concepts: Understanding of the rendering pipeline, framebuffers, etc.

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟🌟 (Intermediate)

The beginner portion is very friendly; advanced topics (PBR, deferred rendering) are more challenging.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 60-80 hours

  • Foundations: ~20 hours
  • Lighting and materials: ~15 hours
  • Advanced topics: ~25-45 hours

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• English: learnopengl.com
  • Chinese: learnopengl-cn.github.io
  • Source code: GitHub

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Always write code. The core of this tutorial is hands-on practice. After each chapter, run the code immediately, then try modifying parameters and observing the changes.

  2. Don't skip the foundational chapters. The early chapters (OpenGL basics, shaders, textures) may seem simple, but they are the foundation for all advanced topics.

  3. Understand every stage of the rendering pipeline. From vertex input to fragment output — what each stage does and how data flows — this is the most core knowledge in graphics.

  4. Combine with your Godot experience. Godot 4 also uses OpenGL/Vulkan rendering under the hood. After learning LearnOpenGL, you'll better understand Godot's rendering pipeline and shader system.

  5. Pair with 3Blue1Brown's linear algebra. If your math foundation isn't solid, start with 3Blue1Brown's Essence of Linear Algebra series.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Physically Based Rendering: From Theory to Implementation (PBRT) is the authoritative text on offline rendering and path tracing, written by Matt Pharr, Wenzel Jakob, and Greg Humphreys. The book starts from physical principles and systematically explains how to build a physically-based renderer.

PBRT's core philosophy is: rendering should simulate the physical behavior of real light. The book derives the rendering equation, BRDF, Monte Carlo integration, importance sampling, and other core concepts in detail, and provides complete renderer implementation code.

This book is freely available online and fully open-source. It's the best resource for understanding modern rendering techniques (including PBR rendering in film VFX and game engines).

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• C++ programming skills: PBRT is implemented in C++; familiarity with modern C++ features is needed
  • Calculus and probability theory: Monte Carlo integration, probability density functions, and other mathematical tools are core content
  • Linear algebra: Matrix transformations, coordinate system conversions
  • Basic graphics concepts: Recommended to first study ray tracing fundamentals before reading PBRT

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟🌟🌟🌟 (Advanced)

PBRT is one of the most in-depth textbooks in graphics, with dense mathematical derivations. Best suited for advanced study after building a solid foundation.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 100-150 hours (16 chapters, ~6-10 hours each)

  • Reading and understanding: ~60-80 hours
  • Code reading and experimentation: ~40-70 hours

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Online (free): pbrt.org
  • Source code: GitHub
  • Book (3rd edition): Published by Elsevier

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Complete Ray Tracing in One Weekend first. PBRT is advanced material. Build ray tracing intuition through Ray Tracing in One Weekend before diving into PBRT's mathematical derivations.

  2. Focus on understanding Monte Carlo integration. This is the core mathematical tool of path tracing. Understand why Monte Carlo methods can solve the rendering equation, and why importance sampling accelerates convergence.

  3. Follow along with the code. PBRT's code structure is very clear. Read the book while cross-referencing the code, understanding how each class and method maps to theoretical concepts.

  4. Don't try to read it all at once. PBRT is extremely rich in content. Read in stages: Chapters 1-5 (foundations) first, then Chapters 6-10 (light transport), and finally Chapters 11-16 (advanced topics).

  5. Modify the renderer. Try modifying PBRT's code to add new material models or light source types. Use code modifications to verify your understanding of the theory.

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Ray Tracing in One Weekend is a series of ray tracing tutorials written by Peter Shirley, spanning three books. Using a few hundred lines of C++ code, the tutorials guide you through building a complete ray tracer from scratch — achievable in just three days.

This series is the best hands-on project for getting started with graphics. You'll implement: ray generation, object intersection, reflection, refraction, shadows, scattering, camera systems, and acceleration structures. At the end, you'll have a beautifully rendered image produced by your own code — an incredible sense of accomplishment.

The three books progress as follows: - Book 1: Basic ray tracing, building a simple renderer - Book 2: Adding textures, volume rendering, instancing, and BVH acceleration structures - Book 3: Building a complete path tracer supporting global illumination

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• C++ basics: Familiarity with basic syntax is sufficient; no advanced features needed
  • Basic math: Vector operations (addition, subtraction, dot product, cross product) — the tutorial explains these in detail
  • No graphics background required: This tutorial IS your graphics introduction

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
🌟🌟 (Beginner)

The tutorial starts from scratch with detailed mathematical derivations and clean code — the best starting point for learning graphics.

docs.title: Estimated Study Hours
docs.text:
Approximately 20-30 hours (three books, ~7-10 hours each)

  • Book 1: ~7-10 hours
  • Book 2: ~7-10 hours
  • Book 3: ~7-10 hours

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Book 1: Ray Tracing in One Weekend
  • Book 2: Ray Tracing: The Next Week
  • Book 3: Ray Tracing: The Rest of Your Life
  • Source code: GitHub

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
1. Type the code yourself. Don't copy-paste. Type every line and understand what each one does. When you hit errors, try debugging them yourself.

  2. Don't rush. Despite the name "One Weekend," there's no need to finish in a single day. Each chapter is packed with knowledge — digesting slowly is more valuable than speed-running.

  3. Modify parameters and observe changes. Try changing material parameters, light positions, and camera angles. Observing how the rendered output changes is the best way to understand graphics principles.

  4. Draw diagrams to aid understanding. The processes of ray reflection, refraction, and scattering are much easier to understand with diagrams than with text alone.

  5. Try extending after finishing. After completing all three books, try adding your own features: depth of field, motion blur, ambient occlusion. This is the key step from "following along" to "creating on your own."

docs.title: What is Computer Networks
docs.text: Computer networks study how computers reliably exchange data with each other. From browsing webpages to messaging apps to distributed systems — all depend on computer networks.

docs.title: Why Learn Computer Networks
docs.text:
Foundation of all distributed systems. Microservices, cloud computing, distributed databases — all built on networking.

Interview essential. "TCP three-way handshake," "HTTP vs. HTTPS," "DNS resolution" — these appear in virtually every technical interview.

Core subject in CS entrance exams. ~25 points.

docs.title: Network Architecture
docs.text:
• OSI 7-layer model vs. TCP/IP 4-layer model
  • Encapsulation and decapsulation

docs.title: Network Layer
docs.text:
• IP protocol: IPv4, subnetting, CIDR
  • Routing algorithms: Distance Vector (RIP), Link State (OSPF), Path Vector (BGP)

docs.title: Transport Layer
docs.text:
• TCP: Three-way handshake, four-way teardown, sliding window, congestion control
  • UDP: Connectionless, unreliable but efficient

docs.title: Application Layer
docs.text:
• HTTP/HTTPS: Request methods, status codes, headers
  • DNS: Domain resolution process

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Top-down, starting from what you know. Recommended: Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach.

Get hands-on with experiments. Use Wireshark to capture packets and see real TCP handshakes and HTTP requests.

Do the Stanford CS144 labs. Implement a TCP/IP protocol stack.

docs.title: Resources in This Module
docs.text: Resource Type Description Stanford CS144 Course Stanford networking course, implement protocol stack Kurose & Ross Textbook Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach USTC Zheng Quan Course Chinese lectures, great for systematic study

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: James Kurose, Keith Ross (University of Massachusetts)
  • Prerequisites: None, suitable for beginners
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

Computer Networking: A Top-Down Approach is one of the most popular networking textbooks in the world. Its unique feature: starting from the application layer and working down to the lower layers — exactly matching how you experience the network.

The book is known for clear explanations, rich examples, and comprehensive resources. Each chapter has extensive exercises, programming assignments, and Wireshark labs.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Official site: gaia.cs.umass.edu/kurose_ross/
  • Resources: Lecture slides, Wireshark labs, programming assignments
  • Chinese translation: 《计算机网络：自顶向下方法》(7th Edition)

docs.title: Application Layer
docs.text:
• HTTP, DNS, SMTP, P2P
  • Socket programming

docs.title: Transport Layer
docs.text:
• UDP, TCP
  • Reliable data transfer, congestion control

docs.title: Network Layer
docs.text:
• IP protocol, routing algorithms
  • IPv4/IPv6

docs.title: Link Layer
docs.text:
• Ethernet, switches
  • ARP, VLAN

docs.title: Network Security
docs.text:
• Cryptography basics
  • TLS/SSL, firewalls

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Start from the application layer. This is the book's signature approach — begin with HTTP and DNS (what you know), then go deeper.

Do the Wireshark labs. Each chapter has accompanying Wireshark labs that let you see real network packets.

Pair with Stanford CS144. This book covers theory; CS144 provides hands-on labs. Together, they're the perfect combination.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: This is the best textbook for networking beginners. If you only read one networking book, this should be it.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: Stanford University
  • Prerequisites: Computer architecture basics, C++ basics
  • Programming Language: C++
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟🌟
  • Website: cs144.github.io

Stanford CS144 is Stanford's networking course, distinguished by its lab-driven approach. You implement a complete TCP/IP protocol stack — from the network interface layer to the transport layer — and your implementation must pass real network communication tests.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:

docs.title: Lab List
docs.text: Lab Content Difficulty Lab 0: Networking warmup Network programming basics 🌟 Lab 1: Webget Implement a simple HTTP client 🌟🌟 Lab 2: TCP receiver Implement TCP receiver 🌟🌟🌟 Lab 3: TCP sender Implement TCP sender 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 4: TCP connection Complete TCP connection 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 5: Network interface Network interface layer 🌟🌟🌟 Lab 6: Router Implement a router 🌟🌟🌟🌟 Lab 7: Putting it all together Complete protocol stack 🌟🌟🌟🌟🌟

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Solid C++ skills required. The course uses C++; familiarity with pointers, memory management, and OOP is essential.

Do labs in order. Each lab depends on the previous lab's code; you can't skip ahead.

Verify with Wireshark. After each lab, use Wireshark to capture packets and verify your implementation is correct.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: CS144 has some of the best lab design in networking courses. After completing all labs, your understanding of TCP/IP will surpass most practitioners.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Source: University of Science and Technology of China (Zheng Quan, Yang Jian)
  • Prerequisites: Basic computer science
  • Difficulty: 🌟�🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 40+

USTC Professor Zheng Quan's Computer Networks is one of the best networking courses in China. It's systematic and thorough, particularly suitable for graduate entrance exam preparation and systematic study of networking principles.

The course covers the complete networking knowledge system, from the physical layer to the application layer, with emphasis on core protocols at the network and transport layers.

docs.title: Course Resources
docs.text:
• Lectures: Bilibili
  • Slides: Available in Bilibili video descriptions

docs.title: Course Features
docs.text:
• Systematic: Layer-by-layer coverage from physical to application
  • Focused: Network and transport layers are covered in depth
  • Exam-oriented: Content closely matches the 408 entrance exam syllabus
  • Clear Chinese explanations: Fluent language with clear concept explanations

docs.title: Self-Study Tips
docs.text:
Pair with Kurose & Ross. Zheng Quan covers principles; Kurose & Ross provides English perspectives and more examples.

Do the practice problems. The course has accompanying exercises, especially useful for exam prep.

Combine with Wireshark labs. Use Wireshark to verify protocol principles learned in class.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: If you're preparing for the 408 entrance exam, Zheng Quan's networking course is essential viewing. Combined with Kurose & Ross, it covers both theory and exam preparation.

docs.title: What is Software Engineering
docs.text:
Software Engineering is the discipline of systematically building high-quality software. It's not just "writing code" — it covers the entire software lifecycle: requirements analysis, design, implementation, testing, deployment, and maintenance.

When you're working on a course assignment alone, you don't need software engineering. But as projects grow larger, teams get bigger, and maintenance periods get longer, without engineering methods things fall apart — messy code, frequent bugs, and changes that break everything. Software engineering solves these problems.

docs.title: Why Learn Software Engineering
docs.text:
Write maintainable code. You can write code, but will you understand it six months later? Can someone else take it over? Software engineering teaches you to write readable, maintainable, and testable code.

Learn team collaboration. Real-world software is built by teams. Version control, code reviews, task division — these collaboration skills are fundamental for software engineers.

Understand what "good software" means. It's not enough for code to run. Good software should be correct, reliable, efficient, maintainable, and scalable. Software engineering builds this quality awareness.

Interviews and career development. Software engineering skills (code quality, system design, engineering habits) are core evaluation criteria in technical interviews and daily work.

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text:
• Code readability: naming conventions, comments, code style
  • Code Review: how to give feedback on others' code, how to receive feedback
  • Static checking and testing: type checking, lint tools, unit testing, integration testing
  • Technical debt: balancing short-term compromises with long-term costs

docs.title: Specifications and Design
docs.text:
• Specifications: preconditions, postconditions, behavioral equivalence
  • Abstract Data Types (ADTs): encapsulation, representation independence, abstraction functions and representation invariants
  • Interface design: what good interfaces look like, how to design reusable components
  • Immutability: why immutable objects are safer, when to use mutability

docs.title: Version Control and Collaboration
docs.text:
• Git workflows: branching strategies, merge vs rebase, PR/MR workflows
  • Team version control: Git practices for multi-person collaboration
  • Continuous Integration (CI): automated builds and testing

docs.title: Software Design Principles
docs.text:
• Single Responsibility Principle: a class should do one thing
  • Open/Closed Principle: open for extension, closed for modification
  • Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations
  • DRY Principle: Don't Repeat Yourself

docs.title: Concurrency and Safety
docs.text:
• Thread safety: the risks of shared mutable state
  • Locks and synchronization: synchronized, Lock, deadlocks
  • Message passing: using message queues instead of shared memory

docs.title: How to Learn
docs.text:
Start with a good course. Software engineering can't be learned just from books — it requires practice in real projects. MIT 6.031 Software Construction is highly recommended — it covers core software engineering concepts thoroughly with exercises for each topic.

Practice in real projects. Find a project with a few thousand lines of code (an open-source project or a large course assignment) and practice code review, writing tests, and refactoring. Theory without practice stays on paper.

Build engineering habits. Every time you write code, ask yourself: can others understand this? Is it tested? Is there a cleaner way? These habits are more important than any specific technology.

Read excellent code. GitHub has countless high-quality open-source projects. Reading their code structure, naming conventions, and test organization is more valuable than reading ten textbooks.

docs.title: Courses
docs.text:
• MIT 6.031: Software Construction — Highly recommended. 30 readings covering core topics, each with exercises
  • Berkeley CS169: Software Engineering — Focuses on agile development and SaaS architecture
  • CMU 17-313: Foundations of Software Engineering — CMU's software engineering foundations course

docs.title: Books
docs.text:
• Clean Code (Robert Martin) — How to write readable, maintainable code
  • Refactoring (Martin Fowler) — Systematic approaches to code refactoring
  • Design Patterns (GoF) — Classic design pattern reference

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• GitHub — Version control and collaboration platform
  • SonarQube — Code quality and security analysis tool
  • JUnit / pytest — Java / Python testing frameworks

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: UC Berkeley
  • Prerequisites: CS61B (Data Structures) or equivalent
  • Programming Language: Ruby on Rails
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

Berkeley CS169 is Berkeley's software engineering course, focused on agile development and SaaS architecture. The course project requires building a complete SaaS application with Ruby on Rails.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text: • Website: cs169.org

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Agile development: Scrum, TDD, continuous integration
  • SaaS architecture: REST API, microservices, deployment
  • Large project: Build a complete SaaS application

docs.title: Tips
docs.text: Learn Ruby on Rails basics first.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: CS169 is the best course for learning agile development and SaaS architecture.

docs.title: Descriptions
docs.text:
• Offered by: MIT
  • Prerequisites: Python programming basics
  • Programming Language: Java
  • Difficulty: 🌟🌟🌟
  • Class Hour: 60+

MIT 6.031 is MIT's software engineering course, focused on writing high-quality, maintainable code. Covers static checking, testing, specifications, ADTs, concurrency, version control.

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Website: web.mit.edu/6.031
  • Notes: 30 readings covering all core topics

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• 30 readings covering static checking, testing, specs, ADTs, concurrency, version control
  • Accompanying exercises for each reading
  • Java-based but concepts apply to all languages

docs.title: Tips
docs.text: Read all materials in order. Each builds on the previous.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: MIT 6.031 is one of the best courses for learning to write "elegant" code.
